# SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1**
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q:** Define the terms *support*, *confidence*, and *lift* in association rule mining. Explain how lift indicates positive, negative, or zero correlation.

**Model Answer:**

Support measures the frequency of occurrence of an itemset in the database:

$$\text{sup}(X) = \frac{\text{sup}_a(X)}{N}$$

Confidence measures the conditional probability of $Y$ given $X$:

$$\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)}$$

Lift measures correlation by comparing observed co-occurrence to expected under independence:

$$\text{lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{P(X \cap Y)}{P(X) \cdot P(Y)}$$

- $\text{lift} > 1$ ‚Üí **Positive correlation**
- $\text{lift} = 1$ ‚Üí **Independence**
- $\text{lift} < 1$ ‚Üí **Negative correlation**

[3 Marks: 1 mark each for formula + brief interpretation]

---

### **Question A2**
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q:** State and explain the *Apriori Property*. How is it used for candidate pruning?

**Model Answer:**

The **Apriori Property** (also called *anti-monotonicity of support*) states that:

> If an itemset $X$ is frequent, then every subset of $X$ must also be frequent. Equivalently, if any subset of $X$ is infrequent, then $X$ cannot be frequent.

In algorithmic terms, during candidate generation:

$$\forall s \subset c, \ \vert s \vert = k-1, \ \text{if } s \notin L_{k-1} \implies \text{prune } c$$

This drastically reduces the candidate search space ‚ÄĒ a 1000-itemset database at $k=4$ can drop to tens of candidates after pruning.

[3 Marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 for the formula, 1 for pruning consequence]

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *Apriori Algorithm + Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024 Model Question]**

Consider the following transactional database with $\text{min\_sup} = 30\%$ and $\text{min\_conf} = 60\%$.

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| T1 | A, B, C |
| T2 | A, C |
| T3 | A, D |
| T4 | B, C, D |
| T5 | A, B, D |
| T6 | B, D |
| T7 | A, B, C |
| T8 | A, B, D |
| T9 | A, B, C, D |
| T10 | B, C |

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Apply the Apriori algorithm to find all frequent itemsets. Show the candidate generation and pruning steps explicitly.

**Solution:**

$N = 10$, so $\text{min\_sup} = 30\% \times 10 = 3$ transactions.

**Pass 1: $L_1$**

| Item | Support Count | $\geq 3$ ? |
|---|---|---|
| A | T1, T2, T3, T5, T7, T8, T9 ‚Üí **7** | ‚úď |
| B | T1, T4, T5, T6, T7, T8, T9, T10 ‚Üí **8** | ‚úď |
| C | T1, T2, T4, T7, T9, T10 ‚Üí **6** | ‚úď |
| D | T3, T4, T5, T6, T8, T9 ‚Üí **6** | ‚úď |

$$L_1 = \{\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}\}$$

**Pass 2: $C_2$ from $L_1$**

Join: $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ candidates. None can be pruned (all 1-subsets are in $L_1$).

Support counts:

| Itemset | TIDs | Count | $\geq 3$ ? |
|---|---|---|---|
| {A,B} | T1, T5, T7, T8, T9 | 5 | ‚úď |
| {A,C} | T1, T2, T7, T9 | 4 | ‚úď |
| {A,D} | T3, T5, T8, T9 | 4 | ‚úď |
| {B,C} | T1, T4, T7, T9, T10 | 5 | ‚úď |
| {B,D} | T4, T5, T6, T8, T9 | 5 | ‚úď |
| {C,D} | T4, T9 | 2 | ‚úó |

$$L_2 = \{\{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{A,D\}, \{B,C\}, \{B,D\}\}$$

**Pass 3: $C_3$ from $L_2$**

Join produces: $\{A,B,C\}, \{A,B,D\}, \{A,C,D\}, \{B,C,D\}$.

Pruning check:

- $\{A,B,C\}$ ‚Üí subsets $\{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{B,C\}$ all in $L_2$ ‚úď Keep
- $\{A,B,D\}$ ‚Üí subsets $\{A,B\}, \{A,D\}, \{B,D\}$ all in $L_2$ ‚úď Keep
- $\{A,C,D\}$ ‚Üí subset $\{C,D\} \notin L_2$ ‚úó **Prune**
- $\{B,C,D\}$ ‚Üí subset $\{C,D\} \notin L_2$ ‚úó **Prune**

Support counts:

| Itemset | TIDs | Count | $\geq 3$ ? |
|---|---|---|---|
| {A,B,C} | T1, T7, T9 | 3 | ‚úď |
| {A,B,D} | T5, T8, T9 | 3 | ‚úď |

$$L_3 = \{\{A,B,C\}, \{A,B,D\}\}$$

**Pass 4: $C_4$ from $L_3$**

Join: $\{A,B,C,D\}$. Subset $\{A,C,D\} \notin L_3$ ‚Üí **Pruned**.

$C_4 = \varnothing$. **Algorithm terminates.**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{F} = L_1 \cup L_2 \cup L_3}$$

[7 Marks: 1 for L1, 2 for L2, 3 for L3 + pruning, 1 for termination]

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Generate all strong association rules from $L_3$ itemsets and compute the lift for each.

**Solution:**

**Rules from $\{A, B, C\}$** ($\text{sup}_a = 3$, $\text{sup} = 0.3$)

| Rule | Confidence | Calculation | $\geq 60\%$ ? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A \cap B \Rightarrow C$ | $0.3 / 0.5 = 0.60$ | $60\%$ | ‚úď |
| $A \cap C \Rightarrow B$ | $0.3 / 0.4 = 0.75$ | $75\%$ | ‚úď |
| $B \cap C \Rightarrow A$ | $0.3 / 0.5 = 0.60$ | $60\%$ | ‚úď |
| $A \Rightarrow B \cap C$ | $0.3 / 0.7 = 0.43$ | $43\%$ | ‚úó |
| $B \Rightarrow A \cap C$ | $0.3 / 0.8 = 0.375$ | $37.5\%$ | ‚úó |
| $C \Rightarrow A \cap B$ | $0.3 / 0.6 = 0.50$ | $50\%$ | ‚úó |

**Rules from $\{A, B, D\}$** ($\text{sup}_a = 3$, $\text{sup} = 0.3$)

| Rule | Confidence | $\geq 60\%$ ? |
|---|---|---|
| $A \cap B \Rightarrow D$ | $0.3 / 0.5 = 0.60$ | ‚úď |
| $A \cap D \Rightarrow B$ | $0.3 / 0.4 = 0.75$ | ‚úď |
| $B \cap D \Rightarrow A$ | $0.3 / 0.5 = 0.60$ | ‚úď |
| $A \Rightarrow B \cap D$ | $0.3 / 0.7 = 0.43$ | ‚úó |
| $B \Rightarrow A \cap D$ | $0.3 / 0.8 = 0.375$ | ‚úó |
| $D \Rightarrow A \cap B$ | $0.3 / 0.6 = 0.50$ | ‚úó |

**Lift Calculations for Selected Strong Rules:**

For $A \cap B \Rightarrow C$:
$$\text{sup}(C) = 0.6, \quad \text{lift} = \frac{0.60}{0.60} = 1.000 \quad \text{(Independence)}$$

For $A \cap C \Rightarrow B$:
$$\text{sup}(B) = 0.8, \quad \text{lift} = \frac{0.75}{0.80} = 0.9375 \quad \text{(Slight Negative Correlation)}$$

For $A \cap D \Rightarrow B$:
$$\text{sup}(B) = 0.8, \quad \text{lift} = \frac{0.75}{0.80} = 0.9375 \quad \text{(Slight Negative Correlation)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Strong Rules (6):**
> $A \cap B \Rightarrow C$, $A \cap C \Rightarrow B$, $B \cap C \Rightarrow A$,
> $A \cap B \Rightarrow D$, $A \cap D \Rightarrow B$, $B \cap D \Rightarrow A$.

[7 Marks: 4 for confidence table, 2 for lift, 1 for summary]

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ *Alternative Choice: Closed/Maximal Itemsets + Theory*

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Question, Internal Choice]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the differences between **frequent**, **closed**, and **maximal** itemsets. Use a worked example to illustrate.

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions:**

- A **frequent itemset** $X$ satisfies $\text{sup}(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$.
- A **closed frequent itemset** $X$ satisfies: no proper superset $Y \supset X$ has $\text{sup}(Y) = \text{sup}(X)$.
- A **maximal frequent itemset** $X$ satisfies: no proper superset $Y \supset X$ is frequent.

**Worked Example (using earlier database, min_sup = 2):**

| Itemset | Support | Frequent? | Closed? | Maximal? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| {I1} | 6 | ‚úď | ‚úó (superset {I1,I2} also has different support) ‚ÄĒ actually ‚úď | ‚úó |
| {I2} | 7 | ‚úď | ‚úď | ‚úó |
| {I1,I2} | 4 | ‚úď | ‚úď | ‚úó |
| {I1,I2,I3} | 2 | ‚úď | ‚úď | ‚úď |
| {I1,I2,I5} | 2 | ‚úď | ‚úď | ‚úď |

**Hierarchy of Compression:**

$$\text{Maximal Itemsets} \subset \text{Closed Itemsets} \subset \text{All Frequent Itemsets}$$

- **Maximal** = most compact, but loses support information.
- **Closed** = compact *and* lossless (supports can be recovered).
- **All frequent** = complete but largest.

[7 Marks: 2 marks definitions, 3 marks worked table, 2 marks hierarchy diagram]

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **limitations of the Apriori algorithm** and how **FP-Growth** overcomes them.

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of Apriori:**

1. **Repeated database scans** ‚ÄĒ at least $k$ passes are required for the longest frequent itemset of size $k$.
2. **Candidate explosion** ‚ÄĒ even after pruning, $C_k$ can be huge (e.g., $10^4$ itemsets at $k=2$).
3. **Memory inefficient** ‚ÄĒ candidate generation requires storing $C_k$ in memory.
4. **Wasted scans** ‚ÄĒ candidates that turn out infrequent still consume I/O cost.
5. **Poor for low support thresholds** ‚ÄĒ candidate count grows exponentially.

**How FP-Growth Solves These:**

- **Two database scans only**, regardless of $k$.
- Builds a compact **FP-Tree** (Frequent Pattern Tree) storing transactions in a compressed prefix-tree form.
- **No candidate generation** ‚ÄĒ uses a *divide-and-conquer* pattern-fragment growth approach.
- Mines conditional pattern bases recursively.
- Memory-efficient and typically 10√ó faster than Apriori in practice.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Apriori | FP-Growth |
|---|---|---|
| Number of DB scans | $k$ (‚Č• 2) | 2 |
| Candidate generation | Yes | No |
| Memory usage | High (candidates) | Moderate (FP-Tree) |
| Speed | Slow for large $k$ | Fast |
| Threshold sensitivity | Severe at low min_sup | Robust |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Missing the pruning table** ‚ÄĒ Apriori questions often expect an explicit "subset-check" table for $C_k$. Skipping this loses 2‚Äď3 marks.
> - **Forgetting absolute vs relative support** ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 papers may specify either. Always reconfirm: $\text{min\_sup}$ absolute = threshold count; relative = $\frac{\text{count}}{N}$.
> - **Wrong rule direction** ‚ÄĒ A rule $A \Rightarrow B$ has confidence $\frac{\text{sup}(A \cup B)}{\text{sup}(A)}$ *not* $\frac{\text{sup}(A \cup B)}{\text{sup}(B)}$. Students confuse antecedent and consequent support.
> - **Lift < 1 ‚Č† bad rule** ‚ÄĒ Always interpret lift alongside confidence; a rule can have high confidence but lift < 1 (negative correlation).
> - **Not showing termination step** ‚ÄĒ Explicitly state $C_4 = \varnothing$ or whichever generation produces empty candidates.
> - **Ignoring rule generation symmetry** ‚ÄĒ When $\text{conf} = 1$, both $A \Rightarrow B$ and $B \Rightarrow A$ may be strong, but they are *different* rules.

[7 Marks: 3 marks Apriori limitations, 3 marks FP-Growth advantage, 1 mark comparison table]

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

### Core Definitions
- **Itemset** ‚ÄĒ set of items; **k-itemset** ‚ÄĒ set of size $k$.
- **Transaction** ‚ÄĒ a record containing an itemset.
- **Support (relative)** ‚ÄĒ fraction of transactions containing $X$: $\text{sup}(X) = \frac{\text{sup}_a(X)}{N}$.
- **Confidence** ‚ÄĒ $P(Y \mid X) = \frac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)}$.
- **Lift** ‚ÄĒ $\frac{P(X \cap Y)}{P(X) \cdot P(Y)}$; $>1$ positive, $<1$ negative, $=1$ independence.
- **Frequent itemset** ‚ÄĒ $\text{sup}(X) \geq \text{min\_sup}$.
- **Strong rule** ‚ÄĒ both support and confidence thresholds met.

### Apriori Algorithm Essentials
- **Apriori Property**: All subsets of a frequent itemset are frequent.
- **Two-step candidate generation**: **Join** ($L_{k-1} \bowtie L_{k-1}$) then **Prune** (remove if any $(k-1)$-subset not in $L_{k-1}$).
- **Pass structure**: $C_1 \rightarrow L_1 \rightarrow C_2 \rightarrow L_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow C_k \rightarrow L_k$ until $C_k = \varnothing$.
- **Termination** when $C_k = \varnothing$.
- **Rule generation**: For each frequent $l_k$, test all $2^k - 2$ non-trivial rules.

### Special Itemsets
- **Closed**: no superset has equal support.
- **Maximal**: no superset is frequent.
- **Maximal ‚äā Closed ‚äā All Frequent**.

### Algorithm Variants
- **AprioriTID** ‚ÄĒ uses TID-list representation.
- **Partition Algorithm** ‚ÄĒ 2-scan method for memory efficiency.
- **FP-Growth** ‚ÄĒ avoids candidate generation entirely.

### Engineering Insights
- Market basket analysis is the prototypical application.
- Low support thresholds ‚Üí combinatorial explosion ‚Üí use FP-Growth.
- Multi-dimensional associations extend itemsets with categorical/numeric attributes.

### Numerical Problem Heuristics (Exam Tips)
1. Always tabulate $C_k$ support *before* pruning for clarity.
2. Show the **pruning table** for $C_k$ ($k \geq 3$) explicitly.
3. Compute both **support and confidence** for every rule candidate.
4. Use **lift** to discuss correlation quality of strong rules.
5. End with a clear "Algorithm terminates because $C_k = \varnothing$" statement.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes ‚ÄĒ Mining Frequent Patterns**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification: General Approach to Classification, Attribute Selection Measures, Na√Įve Bayesian Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCMT604 - CONCEPTS IN DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Classification: General Approach to Classification, Attribute Selection Measures, Na√Įve Bayesian Classification # MODULE 3 ‚ÄĒ CLASSIFICATION: FOUNDATIONS, ATTRIBUTE SELECTION & NA√ŹVE BAYES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Classification? **Classification** is a two-step supervised learning task in data mining whose objective is to learn a mapping (a *model* or *classifier*) from a set of input attribute vectors to a discrete, predefined output class label. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** *Classification is the process of finding a model (or function) that describes and distinguishes data classes or concepts, for the purpose of being able to use the model to predict the class of objects whose class label is unknown.* Mathematically, given a training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ where each $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is a feature vector and $y_i \in \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ is a class label, the learner induces a function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that minimizes the expected misclassification cost. **Key Terminology (Lock these in for KTU exams):** - **Training Set** ‚Üí labelled dataset used to build the model. - **Test Set** ‚Üí unseen labelled dataset used to evaluate generalization. - **Class Label** ‚Üí the categorical (discrete, unordered) target variable $Y$. - **Predictor / Attribute / Feature** ‚Üí the input variables $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_p$. - **Classifier / Model** ‚Üí the learned function $f(\mathbf{x})$. - **Induction** ‚Üí building the model from training data. - **Deduction** ‚Üí applying the model to new instances. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Sorting Wizard" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition:** Think of classification like training a postal sorting clerk. You show the clerk thousands of previously delivered letters (training data), each tagged with the correct destination city (class label). The clerk picks up on visual cues ‚ÄĒ handwriting style, return address, stamp colour ‚ÄĒ and forms mental rules. When a *new* letter arrives, the clerk uses these rules to instantly route it. That mental rule-book is the **classifier**. ### 1.3 General Two-Step Approach to Classification The KTU syllabus prescribes a canonical pipeline: **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Model Construction (Learning Phase)** 1. Each tuple/sample is assumed to belong to a predefined class (label known). 2. The constructed model is built by analysing database tuples described by attributes. 3. The model is represented as **Classification Rules**, **Decision Trees**, or **Mathematical Formulae** (e.g., Bayes' posterior). **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Model Usage (Prediction Phase)** 1. For unseen tuples, the model predicts the class label. 2. Accuracy of the model is estimated using a **Test Set** (held-out labelled data). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Favourite Line:** *A classifier is evaluated on three axes ‚ÄĒ Predictive Accuracy, Speed (computational cost), and Robustness (handling noise & missing values).* ### 1.4 Decision Tree Classification ‚ÄĒ The General Approach A **Decision Tree** is a flowchart-like tree structure where: - Each **internal (non-leaf) node** denotes a test on an attribute $X_j$. - Each **branch** represents an outcome of the test. - Each **leaf node** holds a class label $C_k$. The classic algorithm is the **Hunt's Algorithm** (the conceptual foundation of ID3, C4.5, CART): Let $D_t$ be the set of training records at node $t$. - If $D_t$ contains records belonging to a single class $y_t$, then $t$ is a **leaf node** labelled $y_t$. - If $D_t$ is empty, $t$ is a **leaf node** whose label is the majority class of its parent. - If $D_t$ contains records from multiple classes, select an **attribute test** that partitions $D_t$ into smaller, purer subsets. Recurse on each subset. ### 1.5 Why Attribute Selection Measures? When a node is "impure" (contains a mix of classes), we must choose *which attribute to split on*. Different attributes yield different trees. **Attribute Selection Measures (ASM)** are quantitative heuristics used to rank attributes by their ability to separate the training tuples according to class. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three ASMs you must master for KTU:** > 1. **Information Gain (ID3)** ‚ÄĒ based on Shannon Entropy. > 2. **Gain Ratio (C4.5)** ‚ÄĒ extension that penalises Information Gain. > 3. **Gini Index (CART)** ‚ÄĒ based on impurity, no logarithmic computations. ### 1.6 What is Na√Įve Bayesian Classification? > [!NOTE] > **Na√Įve Bayes** is a *probabilistic classifier* based on applying **Bayes' Theorem** with a **strong (na√Įve) assumption of conditional independence* between every pair of features given the class label. Despite the (often violated) independence assumption, Na√Įve Bayes classifiers have worked surprisingly well in many real-world situations ‚ÄĒ famously in **text classification, spam filtering, and medical diagnosis**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Attribute Selection Measures ‚ÄĒ The Math Behind the Splits ### 2.1.1 Information Gain (ID3 Algorithm) **Shannon Entropy** measures the impurity (or disorder) in a sample set $S$: $$\text{Entropy}(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$ where: - $c$ = number of distinct class labels - $p_i$ = proportion of tuples in $S$ belonging to class $C_i$ - Convention: $0 \log_2 0 = 0$ **Information Gain** of attribute $A$ is the *expected reduction in entropy* caused by partitioning $S$ on attribute $A$: $$\text{Gain}(S, A) = \text{Entropy}(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot \text{Entropy}(S_v)$$ where: - $\text{Values}(A)$ = the set of all possible values of attribute $A$ - $S_v$ = subset of $S$ where attribute $A = v$ - $\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ = weight (probability) of the $v$-th partition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Rule:** The attribute with the **highest Information Gain** is selected as the splitting attribute at each node. Quinlan's ID3 uses exactly this rule. **Limitation of Information Gain:** It is **biased toward attributes with many values** (e.g., a unique ID column would dominate). This is why C4.5 introduced Gain Ratio. ### 2.1.2 Gain Ratio (C4.5 Algorithm) Gain Ratio normalises Information Gain using the *Split Information* (intrinsic value of the split itself): $$\text{SplitInfo}_A(S) = -\sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2\!\left(\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\right)$$ $$\text{GainRatio}(S, A) = \frac{\text{Gain}(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}_A(S)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** C4.5 first computes Information Gain of all candidates, then picks the attribute with the **maximum Gain Ratio** among those with **above-average Gain**. This two-tier heuristic avoids the bias while still preferring informative attributes. ### 2.1.3 Gini Index (CART Algorithm) **Gini Impurity** measures how often a randomly chosen element from $S$ would be incorrectly labelled if labelled randomly according to the class distribution: $$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$$ For a discrete-valued attribute $A$ partitioning $S$ into $k$ subsets, the weighted Gini of the split is: $$\text{Gini}_A(S) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot \text{Gini}(S_v)$$ The attribute that **minimises $\text{Gini}_A(S)$** (equivalently, maximises the *Gini gain* $\Delta = \text{Gini}(S) - \text{Gini}_A(S)$) is chosen. ### 2.1.4 Comparison Table | Property | Information Gain | Gain Ratio | Gini Index | |---|---|---|---| | **Algorithm** | ID3 | C4.5 | CART | | **Math base** | Entropy (log) | Entropy normalised | Squared probabilities | | **Selection rule** | Maximise | Maximise | Minimise impurity | | **Bias** | High-cardinality attributes | Lower bias | Balanced | | **Compute cost** | Slow (logs) | Slow (logs) | Fast (no logs) | | **Output** | Multi-way split | Multi-way split | Binary split only | ## 2.2 Na√Įve Bayesian Classification ‚ÄĒ The Probabilistic Engine ### 2.2.1 Bayes' Theorem (Foundation) For events $H$ (hypothesis) and evidence $E$: $$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$ In classification terms: - $H$ = the class $C$ (e.g., "spam") - $E$ = the observed feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_p)$ $$P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$ To classify, we pick the class with the **maximum posterior**: $$C^* = \arg\max_{C} \; P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{C} \; P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) \cdot P(C)$$ (The denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant across classes, so it can be dropped.) ### 2.2.2 The "Na√Įve" Independence Assumption Computing $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) = P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_p \mid C)$ directly requires estimating a $p$-dimensional joint distribution, which is infeasible for moderate $p$. The **na√Įve assumption** states that the features are conditionally independent given the class: $$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) = \prod_{j=1}^{p} P(x_j \mid C)$$ This gives the final Na√Įve Bayes decision rule: $$\boxed{C_{\text{NB}} = \arg\max_{C} \; P(C) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{p} P(x_j \mid C)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "na√Įve"?** The independence assumption is almost never true in real data (e.g., in spam filtering, the words "free" and "money" are highly correlated). Yet the classifier works surprisingly well ‚ÄĒ this is a celebrated result in machine learning. ### 2.2.3 Estimating the Probabilities **Class Prior:** $$P(C) = \frac{\vert D_C \vert}{\vert D \vert}$$ **Conditional Probability (Categorical / Discrete $x_j$):** $$P(x_j = v \mid C) = \frac{\vert D_{C, x_j = v} \vert}{\vert D_C \vert}$$ **Conditional Probability (Continuous $x_j$, Gaussian NB):** $$P(x_j \mid C) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_C^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_C)^2}{2\sigma_C^2}\right)$$ where $\mu_C$ and $\sigma_C^2$ are the mean and variance of $x_j$ in class $C$. ### 2.2.4 Zero-Frequency Problem & Laplace Smoothing If a categorical attribute value never co-occurs with a class in training, then $P(x_j = v \mid C) = 0$, which **zeros-out the entire posterior** regardless of other evidence. Solution: **Laplace (additive) smoothing**. $$P(x_j = v \mid C) = \frac{\vert D_{C, x_j = v} \vert + 1}{\vert D_C \vert + k}$$ where $k$ = number of distinct values of $x_j$. Generalised with smoothing parameter $\alpha$: $$P(x_j = v \mid C) = \frac{\vert D_{C, x_j = v} \vert + \alpha}{\vert D_C \vert + \alpha \cdot k}$$ ### 2.2.5 Why Na√Įve Bayes Works Despite Violated Assumptions - Dependencies among attributes of the same class often **cancel out** when comparing posteriors. - Optimal classification requires only that the rank-ordering of $P(C \mid \mathbf{x})$ is preserved ‚ÄĒ a weaker condition than exact probability estimation. - Empirically robust on high-dimensional problems (e.g., text). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | |---|---|---| | Shannon Entropy | $\text{Entropy}(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = class proportion | | Information Gain | $\text{Gain}(S,A) = \text{Entropy}(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\text{Entropy}(S_v)$ | $S_v$ = subset where $A = v$ | | Split Information | $\text{SplitInfo}_A(S) = -\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\log_2\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | intrinsic value of partition | | Gain Ratio | $\text{GainRatio}(S,A) = \frac{\text{Gain}(S,A)}{\text{SplitInfo}_A(S)}$ | C4.5 selector | | Gini Index | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ | CART impurity | | Gini of split | $\text{Gini}_A(S) = \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\text{Gini}(S_v)$ | weighted impurity | | Bayes' Theorem | $P(C \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x}\vert C) P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | posterior computation | | Na√Įve NB rule | $C^* = \arg\max_C P(C)\prod_j P(x_j \vert C)$ | joint under independence | | Laplace smoothing | $P(x_j = v \vert C) = \frac{\vert D_{C,x_j=v}\vert + 1}{\vert D_C \vert + k}$ | avoids zero frequency | | Gaussian NB | $P(x_j \vert C) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_C^2}}e^{-\frac{(x_j-\mu_C)^2}{2\sigma_C^2}}$ | continuous attribute | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Information Gain / Gini Index** ‚Üí embedded in scikit-learn's `DecisionTreeClassifier` (`criterion='entropy'` or `'gini'`). > - **Na√Įve Bayes** ‚Üí dominant baseline in **NLP, sentiment analysis, spam filtering, medical diagnosis, real-time recommender systems** because training and prediction are O(n) ‚ÄĒ extremely fast. > - **Laplace smoothing** ‚Üí also known as *additive smoothing*; foundational in language modelling (NLP). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example ‚ÄĒ Information Gain Computation **Training Set $S$ (10 tuples, binary class {Yes, No}):** | Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No | | 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No | | 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes | | 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes | | 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes | | 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No | | 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes | | 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No | | 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes | | 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes | Counts: **Yes = 6, No = 4.** Total = 10. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute Entropy of $S$ $$\text{Entropy}(S) = -\frac{6}{10}\log_2\!\frac{6}{10} - \frac{4}{10}\log_2\!\frac{4}{10}$$ Numerical values: $\frac{6}{10} = 0.6$, $\frac{4}{10} = 0.4$. $$\log_2(0.6) = -0.737$$ $$\log_2(0.4) = -1.322$$ Therefore: $$\text{Entropy}(S) = -0.6(-0.737) - 0.4(-1.322) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.971 \text{ bits}$$ ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute $\text{Gain}(S, \text{Outlook})$ Outlook has 3 values: Sunny (2 Yes, 3 No), Overcast (4 Yes, 0 No), Rain (3 Yes, 1 No). Wait ‚ÄĒ recompute from table: - **Sunny** ‚Üí tuples {1, 2, 8, 9} = {No, No, No, Yes} ‚Üí 1 Yes, 3 No (4 tuples) - **Overcast** ‚Üí tuples {3, 7} = {Yes, Yes} ‚Üí 2 Yes, 0 No (2 tuples) - **Rain** ‚Üí tuples {4, 5, 6, 10} = {Yes, Yes, No, Yes} ‚Üí 3 Yes, 1 No (4 tuples) Entropies of subsets: $$\text{Entropy}(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = -\frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4} - \frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} = 0.5(2) + 0.75(0.415) = 0.811 \text{ bits}$$ $$\text{Entropy}(S_{\text{Overcast}}) = 0 \text{ (pure)}$$ $$\text{Entropy}(S_{\text{Rain}}) = -\frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4} = 0.811 \text{ bits}$$ Weighted sum: $$\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\text{Entropy}(S_v) = \frac{4}{10}(0.811) + \frac{2}{10}(0) + \frac{4}{10}(0.811) = 0.6488 \text{ bits}$$ $$\text{Gain}(S, \text{Outlook}) = 0.971 - 0.649 = 0.322 \text{ bits}$$ ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute $\text{Gain}(S, \text{Humidity})$ (briefly) Humidity = High: {1, 2, 3, 4, 8} = 2 Yes, 3 No (5 tuples) ‚Üí Entropy = 0.971 Humidity = Normal: {5, 6, 7, 9, 10} = 4 Yes, 1 No (5 tuples) ‚Üí Entropy = 0.722 $$\text{Gain}(S, \text{Humidity}) = 0.971 - \frac{5}{10}(0.971) - \frac{5}{10}(0.722) = 0.971 - 0.847 = 0.124 \text{ bits}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison:** Outlook (0.322) > Humidity (0.124). Therefore **Outlook is chosen** as the root split. ## 3.2 Worked Example ‚ÄĒ Na√Įve Bayesian Classification **Training Data (10 tuples, classifying Play=Yes/No given Outlook, Humidity):** Subset for **Yes** (6 tuples): - Sunny-Normal: 1, Sunny-High: 0, Overcast-High: 1, Overcast-Normal: 1, Rain-High: 1, Rain-Normal: 2 Subset for **No** (4 tuples): - Sunny-High: 3, Sunny-Normal: 0, Overcast-High: 0, Overcast-Normal: 0, Rain-High: 0, Rain-Normal: 1 ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute Priors $$P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6 \qquad P(\text{No}) = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$ ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute Conditionals For class = Yes: $$P(\text{Outlook}=\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167$$ $$P(\text{Humidity}=\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2}{6} \approx 0.333$$ For class = No: $$P(\text{Outlook}=\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750$$ $$P(\text{Humidity}=\text{High} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3}{4} = 0.750$$ ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Classify a New Tuple: $\mathbf{x} = (\text{Sunny}, \text{High})$ Apply the NB rule: $$P(\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{High}\mid\text{Yes}) = 0.6 \times 0.167 \times 0.333 = 0.0333$$ $$P(\text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{No}) \cdot P(\text{High}\mid\text{No}) = 0.4 \times 0.750 \times 0.750 = 0.2250$$ **Comparison:** $0.2250 > 0.0333$. $$\boxed{C^* = \text{No} \;\; (\text{do not play})}$$ ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Normalise Posteriors (for completeness) $$P(\text{Yes}\mid\mathbf{x}) = \frac{0.0333}{0.0333 + 0.2250} = 0.129 \quad (\approx 12.9\%)$$ $$P(\text{No}\mid\mathbf{x}) = \frac{0.2250}{0.0333 + 0.2250} = 0.871 \quad (\approx 87.1\%)$$ ### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Demonstration of Laplace Smoothing (assume Outlook=Rainy in test but absent in Yes class) Raw $P(\text{Rain}\mid\text{Yes}) = 0$ ‚Üí poisons the entire posterior. With Laplace ($k = 3$ for {Sunny, Overcast, Rain}, $\alpha = 1$): $$P(\text{Rain}\mid\text{Yes}) = \frac{3 + 1}{6 + 3} = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.444$$ Now the posterior is no longer zeroed. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ When to Use What? | Method | Best For | Avoid When | |---|---|---| | Information Gain (ID3) | Multi-class nominal data, small trees | Continuous attributes (needs binning), high-cardinality features | | Gain Ratio (C4.5) | When IG is biased, mixed attribute types | Datasets needing very fast splits (still uses log) | | Gini Index (CART) | Continuous/binary splits, large datasets | When probability outputs matter more than class label | | Na√Įve Bayes | High-dimensional, text, real-time, missing values | When feature interactions carry strong signal (use Logistic Regression / Tree ensembles instead) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 General Classification Pipeline (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Labelled Dataset D] --> B[Step 1: Data Preprocessing] B --> B1[Handle Missing Values] B --> B2[Encode Categorical Features] B --> B3[Train / Test Split 70 30] B1 --> C[Step 2: Model Induction Training] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> D{Choose Algorithm} D --> D1[Decision Tree ID3 C4.5 CART] D --> D2[Naive Bayes Bernoulli Multinomial Gaussian] D1 --> E[Step 3: Model Storage] D2 --> E E --> F[Step 4: Prediction on Test Data] F --> G[Step 5: Performance Evaluation] G --> G1[Accuracy Precision Recall F1] G --> G2[Confusion Matrix] G --> G3[ROC AUC] G1 --> H[Deploy or Iterate] G2 --> H G3 --> H ``` ## 4.2 Decision Tree Construction Using Hunt's Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD N0[Root Node: All 10 tuples
Yes = 6, No = 4
Entropy = 0.971] --> A1[Outlook = Sunny] N0 --> A2[Outlook = Overcast] N0 --> A3[Outlook = Rain] A1 --> L1[Leaf: No

Entropy = 0.811] N1 --> A4[Humidity = High] N1 --> A5[Humidity = Normal] A4 --> L3[Leaf: No
1 No, 0 Yes Pure] A5 --> L4[Leaf: Yes
2 Yes, 0 No Pure] ``` ## 4.3 Bayes' Theorem Information Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P["Prior P(C)
Class frequency in training"] --> MULT["Multiply"] L["Likelihood P(x|C)
Feature distribution given class"] --> MULT MULT --> POST["Posterior P(C|x)
Naive Bayes Output"] NORM["P(x)
Evidence constant"] --> POST POST --> DEC["argmax over C
Pick class with highest posterior"] ``` ## 4.4 Information Gain Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start: S has mixed classes] --> E[Compute Entropy S] E --> LOOP{For each attribute A in S} LOOP -->|A1| G1[Compute Gain S A1] LOOP -->|A2| G2[Compute Gain S A2] LOOP -->|A3| G3[Compute Gain S A3] G1 --> RANK[Rank attributes by Gain] G2 --> RANK G3 --> RANK RANK --> BEST[Choose attribute with maximum Gain] BEST --> SPLIT[Partition S on chosen attribute] SPLIT --> REC[Recurse on each partition] REC --> STOP{All partitions pure or stopping criterion met} STOP -->|Yes| LEAF[Create leaf with majority class] STOP -->|No| LOOP ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023] Define classification in data mining. Outline its general approach.** **Model Answer:** Classification is a data mining technique used to predict the categorical class label of a given input tuple. It is a two-step process: **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Model Construction (Learning):** - A classifier is built from a training set of tuples, each labelled with a predefined class. - The learned model is represented as classification rules, decision trees, or mathematical formulae. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Model Usage (Classification):** - The model is applied to test data to estimate classification accuracy. - If accuracy is acceptable, the model is used to classify future tuples with unknown labels. Performance is measured using **Predictive Accuracy, Speed, Robustness, Scalability, and Interpretability**. *(3 marks ‚ÄĒ full credit if both steps are clearly named.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024] What is the "na√Įve" assumption in Na√Įve Bayesian Classification? Why is it called na√Įve?** **Model Answer:** The "na√Įve" assumption is that **all attributes are conditionally independent of one another given the class label**. Formally, for features $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_p$ and class $C$: $$P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_p \mid C) = \prod_{j=1}^{p} P(x_j \mid C)$$ It is called "na√Įve" because this assumption is **almost never true in real-world data** ‚ÄĒ attributes are usually correlated. For example, in text classification, words like "free" and "offer" frequently co-occur. Despite this unrealistic simplification, the classifier often delivers surprisingly strong predictive performance, which is why the assumption is labelled as "na√Įve". *(3 marks ‚ÄĒ formula + intuitive justification expected.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model Paper] (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain any two attribute selection measures used in decision tree induction with suitable mathematical formulations. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Consider the following training dataset for the "Play Tennis" problem. Compute the **Information Gain** for the attribute **Outlook** and decide which attribute should be selected as the root of the decision tree. *(7 marks)* | Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play | |-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------| | 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No | | 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No | | 3 | Overcast| Hot | High | Weak | Yes | | 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes | | 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes | | 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No | | 7 | Overcast| Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes | | 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No | | 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes | | 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes | ### **Model Solution (a) ‚ÄĒ Two Attribute Selection Measures** **1. Information Gain (ID3):** Information Gain is the expected reduction in entropy (impurity) caused by partitioning the training set $S$ on attribute $A$. $$\text{Gain}(S, A) = \text{Entropy}(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\,\text{Entropy}(S_v)$$ where $$\text{Entropy}(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$$ The attribute that maximises $\text{Gain}(S, A)$ is selected. *Drawback:* It is biased toward attributes with many distinct values (e.g., a unique ID column). Algorithm: **ID3**. **2. Gini Index (CART):** Gini measures the impurity of a set as the probability of misclassification if labels were assigned randomly: $$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$$ The weighted Gini of a split on attribute $A$ is: $$\text{Gini}_A(S) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\,\text{Gini}(S_v)$$ The attribute that **minimises** $\text{Gini}_A(S)$ is selected. *Advantages:* No logarithms ‚Üí faster; works well on continuous attributes via binary splits. Algorithm: **CART**. *(7 marks ‚ÄĒ 3.5 marks for each ASM with formula and algorithm reference.)* --- ### **Model Solution (b) ‚ÄĒ Information Gain of Outlook** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Class Distribution:** $S$ has 10 tuples: 6 Yes, 4 No. $$\text{Entropy}(S) = -\frac{6}{10}\log_2\frac{6}{10} - \frac{4}{10}\log_2\frac{4}{10} = 0.971 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Partition by Outlook:** | Outlook | Yes | No | Count | Subset Entropy | |---------|-----|-----|-------|----------------| | Sunny | 1 | 3 | 4 | 0.811 | | Overcast| 2 | 0 | 2 | 0.000 | | Rain | 3 | 1 | 4 | 0.811 | Subset entropies (illustrated for Sunny): $$-\tfrac{1}{4}\log_2\tfrac{1}{4} - \tfrac{3}{4}\log_2\tfrac{3}{4} = 0.5 + 0.311 = 0.811 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Weighted average entropy after split:** $$\tfrac{4}{10}(0.811) + \tfrac{2}{10}(0.000) + \tfrac{4}{10}(0.811) = 0.3244 + 0.0000 + 0.3244 = 0.6488 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Information Gain:** $$\text{Gain}(S, \text{Outlook}) = 0.971 - 0.6488 = 0.3222 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Conclusion:** Comparing with other attributes (computed similarly: $\text{Gain}(\text{Humidity}) = 0.124$, $\text{Gain}(\text{Wind}) = 0.029$, $\text{Gain}(\text{Temperature}) = 0.029$), **Outlook has the highest Information Gain** and should be selected as the **root** of the decision tree. *(7 marks ‚ÄĒ distribution of marks: 1 for entropy, 2 for partition, 2 for weighted sum, 1 for gain, 1 for selection.)* --- ### **Question B ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024 Model Paper] (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the **Na√Įve Bayesian Classification** method. State and justify the na√Įve assumption. Derive the decision rule mathematically. *(7 marks)* **(b)** For the dataset in Q-A, classify the tuple $\mathbf{x} = (\text{Outlook} = \text{Sunny}, \text{Humidity} = \text{High})$ using Na√Įve Bayes. Use Laplace smoothing wherever applicable. Show all steps including the computation of priors and likelihoods. *(7 marks)* ### **Model Solution (a) ‚ÄĒ Na√Įve Bayes Theory** **Definition:** Na√Įve Bayesian Classification is a probabilistic supervised learning method based on Bayes' Theorem with a strong conditional independence assumption among features given the class. **Bayes' Theorem:** $$P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C)\,P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$ **Na√Įve Assumption:** All features are conditionally independent given the class: $$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) = \prod_{j=1}^{p} P(x_j \mid C)$$ **Derivation of the Decision Rule:** The predicted class $C^*$ maximises the posterior $P(C \mid \mathbf{x})$: $$C^* = \arg\max_C P(C \mid \mathbf{x})$$ Substituting Bayes' theorem: $$C^* = \arg\max_C \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C)\,P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$ Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ does not depend on $C$: $$C^* = \arg\max_C P(\mathbf{x} \mid C)\,P(C) = \arg\max_C P(C)\prod_{j=1}^{p} P(x_j \mid C)$$ **Justification of "na√Įve":** Real-world features are almost always correlated. For example, in spam detection, "free" and "viagra" co-occur more often than chance. The assumption is therefore statistically unrealistic ‚ÄĒ hence the name "na√Įve". Yet empirically the classifier works extremely well in high-dimensional problems because incorrect independence assumptions often cancel out in posterior comparisons. *(7 marks ‚ÄĒ 2 for definition, 2 for assumption, 2 for derivation, 1 for justification.)* --- ### **Model Solution (b) ‚ÄĒ Na√Įve Bayes Classification with Laplace Smoothing** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Class Priors:** $$P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6 \qquad P(\text{No}) = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Likelihoods (with Laplace smoothing, $k = 3$ for Outlook, $k = 2$ for Humidity, $\alpha = 1$):** | Feature Value | Class | Count | $\vert D_C \vert$ | Smoothed $P(\cdot \mid C)$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Outlook=Sunny | Yes | 1 | 6 | $\frac{1+1}{6+3} = \frac{2}{9} \approx 0.222$ | | Outlook=Sunny | No | 3 | 4 | $\frac{3+1}{4+3} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.571$ | | Humidity=High | Yes | 2 | 6 | $\frac{2+1}{6+2} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$ | | Humidity=High | No | 3 | 4 | $\frac{3+1}{4+2} = \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.667$ | **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Posterior Products:** For $C = \text{Yes}$: $$P(\text{Yes})\,P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{Yes})\,P(\text{High}\mid\text{Yes}) = 0.6 \times 0.222 \times 0.375 = 0.0500$$ For $C = \text{No}$: $$P(\text{No})\,P(\text{Sunny}\mid\text{No})\,P(\text{High}\mid\text{No}) = 0.4 \times 0.571 \times 0.667 = 0.1524$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Decision:** $$0.1524 > 0.0500 \implies C^* = \text{No} \; (\text{do not play})$$ Normalised posteriors: $$P(\text{Yes}\mid\mathbf{x}) = \frac{0.0500}{0.0500 + 0.1524} = 0.247 \quad (24.7\%)$$ $$P(\text{No}\mid\mathbf{x}) = \frac{0.1524}{0.0500 + 0.1524} = 0.753 \quad (75.3\%)$$ **Conclusion:** The tuple is classified as **No ‚ÄĒ do not play tennis**, with 75.3% posterior confidence. *(7 marks ‚ÄĒ 1.5 priors, 2.5 likelihoods with smoothing, 2 posterior products + decision, 1 normalised output.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Before Writing the Exam):** > 1. **Forgetting the convention** $0 \log_2 0 = 0$ ‚ÄĒ losing 0.5 marks when computing entropy of a pure subset. > 2. **Using raw counts instead of proportions** in $p_i$ for entropy / Gini ‚ÄĒ examiners will deduct full marks. > 3. **Confusing *maximise Gain* with *minimise Gini*** ‚ÄĒ the rules are opposites! Write clearly: *ID3 = max gain*, *CART = min Gini*. > 4. **Dropping the denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ incorrectly in Na√Įve Bayes** ‚ÄĒ it must be removed only because it is *constant* across classes, not because it is zero. > 5. **Omitting Laplace smoothing** in NB computation when a feature-class pair has zero count. The posterior collapses to zero ‚ÄĒ examiners expect the smoothed formula. > 6. **Computing $\log_2$ in decimal vs natural log** ‚ÄĒ KTU strictly expects $\log_2$ in ID3/C4.5 entropy. Using $\ln$ will mark the entire problem wrong. > 7. **Not normalising posteriors in NB** when the question asks for a probability ‚ÄĒ present both raw and normalised outputs. > 8. **Wrong "na√Įve" justification** ‚ÄĒ the assumption is na√Įve because it is *mathematically unrealistic*, not because it is "simple" or "fast". --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Pin These in Memory Before the Exam:** - **Classification** is *supervised* learning whose target $Y$ is **categorical** (not continuous ‚ÄĒ that is regression). - The general approach has **two steps**: model construction (induction) and model usage (deduction). - **Hunt's Algorithm** is the conceptual basis for ID3, C4.5, and CART. - **Information Gain** = Entropy($S$) ‚ąí Weighted entropy of partitions. **Choose the attribute with maximum Gain.** Algorithm: **ID3**. - **Gain Ratio** = Information Gain / SplitInfo. **C4.5** uses this; it overcomes ID3's bias toward high-cardinality attributes. - **Gini Index** = $1 - \sum p_i^2$. **CART** uses this; **choose attribute with minimum Gini** (fast ‚ÄĒ no logarithms). - **Bayes' Theorem** has 4 terms: Prior, Likelihood, Evidence, Posterior. Posterior ‚ąĚ Prior √ó Likelihood. - **Na√Įve Bayes** decision rule: $C^* = \arg\max_C P(C)\prod_{j=1}^{p} P(x_j \mid C)$. - **Na√Įve assumption** = conditional independence of features given class ‚ÄĒ almost always violated, yet classifier works. - **Laplace smoothing** formula: $P(x_j = v \mid C) = \frac{\vert D_{C, x_j = v}\vert + 1}{\vert D_C \vert + k}$ ‚ÄĒ prevents zero-frequency catastrophe. - **Gaussian Na√Įve Bayes** is used for continuous attributes; **Multinomial NB** for text/count data; **Bernoulli NB** for binary features. - **Classifier evaluation** uses **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, Confusion Matrix**, and **ROC-AUC**. - **Confusion Matrix** = 2√ó2 grid: TP (True Positive), FN (False Negative), FP (False Positive), TN (True Negative). - $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ - $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ - $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ - $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ - **Na√Įve Bayes Advantages:** fast (linear time), handles missing values, works with high-dimensional data, needs small training set. **Disadvantages:** unrealistic independence, cannot learn interactions, probability estimates can be poor (but ranks are good). - **Decision Tree Advantages:** interpretable, handles mixed attribute types, no normalisation needed. **Disadvantages:** prone to overfitting (use pruning), unstable to small data changes (use ensembles). - **Gini vs Entropy** ‚ÄĒ both measure impurity, Gini is faster (no log), Entropy has stronger theoretical grounding in information theory.

topics.title: Clustering: K-Means, Agglomerative versus Divisive Hierarchical Clustering, BIRCH, DBSCAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT604 - CONCEPTS IN DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clustering: K-Means, Agglomerative versus Divisive Hierarchical Clustering, BIRCH, DBSCAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Clustering

> [!NOTE]
> **Clustering** is an **unsupervised learning** technique in data analytics that partitions a set of $N$ unlabeled data objects into $K$ groups (clusters) such that objects within the same cluster exhibit high **intra-cluster similarity**, while objects in different clusters show high **inter-cluster dissimilarity**.

Mathematically, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, clustering aims to find an assignment function $C: \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ such that a chosen objective function is optimized.

$$J(C) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \mathcal{S}(x_i, \mu_k)$$

where $\mu_k$ is the representative (centroid/prototype) of cluster $C_k$, and $\mathcal{S}(\cdot)$ is a chosen similarity/dissimilarity measure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Library Organizer"**
> Imagine you are a librarian given 10,000 unsorted books with no genre labels. You have no instruction manual. What do you do? You group books that *look* similar ‚ÄĒ by cover color, size, or topic clues. This is exactly what clustering does with data. It finds **natural groupings** without being told the answers in advance.

A second powerful analogy: **Sorting Laundry.** A person sorting clothes intuitively creates clusters ‚ÄĒ whites, darks, delicates, towels ‚ÄĒ based on **feature similarity** (color, fabric, weight). Each new garment is placed in the cluster it most resembles. This mirrors the cluster-assignment step in algorithms like K-Means.

### 1.3 Categories of Clustering Algorithms

Clustering algorithms covered in this module fall into four major families:

| Family | Core Idea | Algorithms Covered |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Partitioning** | Divide data into $K$ non-overlapping clusters | **K-Means** |
| **Hierarchical** | Build a tree (dendrogram) of nested clusters | **Agglomerative** (bottom-up) and **Divisive** (top-down) |
| **Hierarchical (Incremental)** | Build CF-tree for large datasets | **BIRCH** |
| **Density-Based** | Clusters are dense regions separated by sparse areas | **DBSCAN** |

### 1.4 Why Clustering Matters in Data Analytics

> [!NOTE]
> Clustering is foundational in **customer segmentation**, **anomaly detection**, **document organization**, **image compression**, **gene expression analysis**, and **social network analysis**. It is a **core unsupervised technique** that frequently appears in KTU board examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 K-Means Clustering

**K-Means** is a **centroid-based partitioning** algorithm proposed by MacQueen (1967) that iteratively refines cluster assignments to minimize the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**.

#### Operational Steps
1. **Initialization**: Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ (randomly or via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment Step**: Assign each data point $x_i$ to the cluster with the nearest centroid using Euclidean distance.
3. **Update Step**: Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to that cluster.
4. **Convergence Check**: Stop when centroids no longer change (or change below threshold $\epsilon$), or after $T$ iterations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it works**: Each iteration monotonically decreases the objective $J$. The algorithm is guaranteed to **converge** but may converge to a **local minimum**, not the global optimum.

#### K-Means Objective Function

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$$

where $\mu_k = \frac{1}{|C_k|} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$.

### 2.2 Agglomerative vs. Divisive Hierarchical Clustering

**Hierarchical clustering** produces a nested sequence of partitions represented as a **dendrogram** (tree diagram).

| Aspect | Agglomerative (Bottom-Up) | Divisive (Top-Down) |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| **Direction** | Starts with $N$ singleton clusters; merges closest pairs | Starts with one cluster containing all points; recursively splits |
| **Strategy** | Each point begins as its own cluster | All points begin in one cluster |
| **Decision** | Which two clusters to **merge** | Which cluster to **split** |
| **Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ naive, $\mathcal{O}(N^2 \log N)$ with heap | $\mathcal{O}(2^N)$ (typically infeasible) |
| **Most Common** | Yes (used in practice) | Rare (exponential cost) |
| **Output** | Dendrogram | Dendrogram |

#### Linkage Criteria (Inter-Cluster Distance)

For two clusters $A$ and $B$ with sizes $n_A$ and $n_B$:

$$\text{Single Linkage: } d(A, B) = \min_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a, b)$$

$$\text{Complete Linkage: } d(A, B) = \max_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a, b)$$

$$\text{Average Linkage: } d(A, B) = \frac{1}{n_A \cdot n_B} \sum_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a, b)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Single linkage** tends to produce **chained, elongated clusters**, while **complete linkage** produces **compact, spherical clusters** and is more robust to noise.

### 2.3 BIRCH (Balanced Iterative Reducing and Clustering using Hierarchies)

**BIRCH** is designed for **very large datasets** that cannot fit in memory. It builds a **Clustering Feature (CF) tree** in a single scan and then applies a global/secondary clustering on leaf nodes.

#### Clustering Feature (CF) Vector

A CF vector is a **3-tuple**: $\text{CF} = (N, \text{LS}, \text{SS})$ where:
- $N$ = number of data points in the subcluster
- $\text{LS} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ (linear sum)
- $\text{SS} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2$ (squared sum)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CF vector is **additive** ‚ÄĒ combining two disjoint sub-clusters $A$ and $B$:
> $$\text{CF}_A + \text{CF}_B = (N_A + N_B, \text{LS}_A + \text{LS}_B, \text{SS}_A + \text{SS}_B)$$

#### Key CF-Tree Parameters
- **Branching factor $B$**: max children per non-leaf node
- **Threshold $T$**: max radius/diameter of subclusters in leaf
- **Leaf size $L$**: max number of CF entries per leaf

#### BIRCH Phases
1. **Phase 1**: Build CF-tree (single pass over data)
2. **Phase 2** (optional): Condense tree
3. **Phase 3**: Global clustering on leaf entries (e.g., K-Means or agglomerative)
4. **Phase 4** (optional): Refinement by redistributing points

### 2.4 DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)

**DBSCAN** groups together points that are **densely packed** and marks points in low-density regions as **outliers**. It is based on two parameters: **$\epsilon$ (epsilon)** ‚ÄĒ the neighborhood radius, and **MinPts** ‚ÄĒ the minimum number of points to form a dense region.

#### Core Definitions

- **$\epsilon$-neighborhood** of point $p$: $N_\epsilon(p) = \{q \in \mathcal{D} \mid d(p, q) \leq \epsilon\}$
- **Core Point**: $|N_\epsilon(p)| \geq \text{MinPts}$
- **Border Point**: $|N_\epsilon(p)| < \text{MinPts}$ but lies within $\epsilon$ of a core point
- **Noise Point (Outlier)**: Neither core nor border

#### Reachability & Connectivity
- **Directly density-reachable**: $p$ is core, $q \in N_\epsilon(p)$
- **Density-reachable**: Chain of directly density-reachable points
- **Density-connected**: Points connected via shared core point

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DBSCAN can find **arbitrarily shaped clusters** (unlike K-Means), is **robust to outliers**, and does **not require** specifying the number of clusters in advance. However, it struggles with **varying densities** and is **sensitive to $\epsilon$ and MinPts**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---------|----------------------|-------|
| **K-Means Objective** | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Minimized iteratively |
| **Centroid Update** | $\mu_k = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | Mean of assigned points |
| **Euclidean Distance** | $d(p, q) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(p_j - q_j)^2}$ | Default in K-Means |
| **Manhattan Distance** | $d(p, q) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert p_j - q_j \vert$ | L1 norm |
| **Minkowski Distance** | $d(p, q) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert p_j - q_j \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized |
| **Single Linkage** | $d(A, B) = \min \ d(a, b)$ | Tends to chain |
| **Complete Linkage** | $d(A, B) = \max \ d(a, b)$ | Compact clusters |
| **Average Linkage** | $d(A, B) = \dfrac{1}{n_A n_B} \sum d(a, b)$ | Compromise |
| **CF Vector** | $\text{CF} = (N, \text{LS}, \text{SS})$ | Additive property |
| **Centroid from CF** | $\mu = \text{LS} / N$ | Recovered from CF |
| **Radius from CF** | $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SS}}{N} - \left( \dfrac{\text{LS}}{N} \right)^2}$ | Controls threshold $T$ |
| **DBSCAN Neighborhood** | $N_\epsilon(p) = \{q : d(p, q) \leq \epsilon\}$ | Core if $\vert N_\epsilon \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$ |
| **WCSS (Inertia)** | $\text{WCSS} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Lower is better |
| **Silhouette Coefficient** | $s(i) = \dfrac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **K-Means** is used in **image compression (vector quantization)**, **customer segmentation** in retail, and **document clustering**.
- **Hierarchical clustering** is used in **gene expression analysis** (phylogenetic trees), **taxonomy creation**, and **social network analysis**.
- **BIRCH** is used in **streaming analytics** and **IoT data pipelines** where memory is a constraint.
- **DBSCAN** is used in **GPS trajectory analysis**, **fraud detection** (anomaly isolation), and **geospatial hotspot detection**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 K-Means: Mathematical Derivation of Centroid Update Rule

We want to find $\mu_k$ that minimizes $J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$.

Expanding the squared norm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_k &= \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k)^\top (x_i - \mu_k) \\
&= \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \left( x_i^\top x_i - 2 x_i^\top \mu_k + \mu_k^\top \mu_k \right) \\
&= \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i^\top x_i \;-\; 2 \mu_k^\top \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i \;+\; n_k \cdot \mu_k^\top \mu_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1**: Take the partial derivative of $J_k$ with respect to $\mu_k$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial \mu_k} = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i + 2 n_k \mu_k = 0
$$

**Step 2**: Solve for $\mu_k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 n_k \mu_k &= 2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i \\
\mu_k &= \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: The optimal centroid is the **arithmetic mean** of the points in the cluster.

### 3.2 DBSCAN: Reachability and Connectivity Derivations

**Direct Density-Reachability** ‚ÄĒ $q$ is directly density-reachable from $p$ iff:
- $p \in \text{Core Points}$ (i.e., $|N_\epsilon(p)| \geq \text{MinPts}$)
- $q \in N_\epsilon(p)$ (i.e., $d(p, q) \leq \epsilon$)

**Density-Reachability** ‚ÄĒ $q$ is density-reachable from $p$ if there exists a chain of points $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m$ such that:

$$
p = p_1 \rightarrow p_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow p_m = q
$$

where each $p_{i+1}$ is directly density-reachable from $p_i$. Note: density-reachability is **asymmetric** (a core point can reach a border point, but not vice-versa).

**Density-Connectivity** ‚ÄĒ $p$ and $q$ are density-connected if there exists a point $o$ such that both $p$ and $q$ are density-reachable from $o$. Connectivity is **symmetric** and **reflexive**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **cluster** in DBSCAN is defined as the **maximal set of density-connected points**.

### 3.3 BIRCH: CF Vector Recovery of Centroid and Radius

Given a CF vector $\text{CF} = (N, \text{LS}, \text{SS})$:

**Centroid derivation**:

$$
\mu = \frac{\text{LS}}{N} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i
$$

**Radius derivation** (average squared distance from centroid):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \| x_i - \mu \|^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( x_i^\top x_i - 2 \mu^\top x_i + \mu^\top \mu \right) \\
&= \frac{\text{SS}}{N} - 2 \mu^\top \mu + \mu^\top \mu \\
&= \frac{\text{SS}}{N} - \| \mu \|^2 \\
&= \frac{\text{SS}}{N} - \left( \frac{\text{LS}}{N} \right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R = \sqrt{ \frac{\text{SS}}{N} - \left( \frac{\text{LS}}{N} \right)^2 }
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The radius is used to enforce the **threshold $T$** in the leaf nodes. If inserting a new point would cause $R > T$, a new CF entry is created.

### 3.4 Hierarchical Clustering: Linkage-Based Update Demonstration

Suppose clusters $A$ and $B$ are merged into cluster $A \cup B$. The distance to a third cluster $C$ must be updated. For **single linkage**:

$$
d(A \cup B, C) = \min_{z \in A \cup B, \, c \in C} d(z, c) = \min\{ d(A, C), d(B, C) \}
$$

For **complete linkage**:

$$
d(A \cup B, C) = \max\{ d(A, C), d(B, C) \}
$$

For **average linkage** (Lance-Williams formula generalization):

$$
d(A \cup B, C) = \frac{n_A}{n_A + n_B} d(A, C) + \frac{n_B}{n_A + n_B} d(B, C)
$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example ‚ÄĒ K-Means on 4 Points

Let $\mathcal{D} = \{(1, 1), (1, 2), (8, 8), (9, 9)\}$ with $K = 2$ and initial centroids $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$, $\mu_2 = (8, 8)$.

**Iteration 1 ‚ÄĒ Assignment Step**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d((1,1), \mu_1) &= 0, & d((1,1), \mu_2) &= \sqrt{98} \approx 9.90 \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 \\
d((1,2), \mu_1) &= 1, & d((1,2), \mu_2) &= \sqrt{85} \approx 9.22 \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 \\
d((8,8), \mu_1) &= 9.90, & d((8,8), \mu_2) &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 \\
d((9,9), \mu_1) &= 11.31, & d((9,9), \mu_2) &= \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1 ‚ÄĒ Update Step**:

$$
\mu_1 = \left( \frac{1+1}{2}, \frac{1+2}{2} \right) = (1, 1.5), \quad \mu_2 = \left( \frac{8+9}{2}, \frac{8+9}{2} \right) = (8.5, 8.5)
$$

**Iteration 2 ‚ÄĒ Assignment**: Reassign points; the assignments remain unchanged. Centroids remain unchanged. **Converged**.

**Final WCSS**:

$$
J = 0.5^2 + 0.5^2 + 0.5^2 + 0.5^2 = 1.0
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 K-Means Algorithm Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Dataset D] --> B[Choose K and Initialize K Centroids]
    B --> C[Assignment Step: Assign each point to nearest centroid]
    C --> D[Update Step: Recompute centroids as cluster means]
    D --> E{Centroids Changed?}
    E -->|Yes| C
    E -->|No| F[Output: Final Clusters and Centroids]
    F --> G[End]
```

### 4.2 Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering ‚ÄĒ Dendrogram Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Point A] --> M1[Cluster 1]
    L2[Point B] --> M1
    L3[Point C] --> M2[Cluster 2]
    L4[Point D] --> M2
    L5[Point E] --> M3[Cluster 3]
    M1 --> N1[Merge 1 and 2 at height h2]
    M2 --> N1
    M3 --> N2[Final Single Cluster at height h3]
    N1 --> N2
```

**Interpretation**: The vertical axis represents the **distance/dissimilarity** at which clusters merge. Cutting the dendrogram horizontally at a chosen height yields the desired partition.

### 4.3 BIRCH ‚ÄĒ CF-Tree Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Root Node] --> N1[Non-Leaf Node 1]
    R --> N2[Non-Leaf Node 2]
    N1 --> L1[Leaf 1 with CF entries]
    N1 --> L2[Leaf 2 with CF entries]
    N2 --> L3[Leaf 3 with CF entries]
    N2 --> L4[Leaf 4 with CF entries]
    L1 --> E1[Entry CF1 = N1 LS1 SS1]
    L1 --> E2[Entry CF2 = N2 LS2 SS2]
    L2 --> E3[Entry CF3 = N3 LS3 SS3]
    L3 --> E4[Entry CF4 = N4 LS4 SS4]
    L4 --> E5[Entry CF5 = N5 LS5 SS5]
```

**Path insertion rule**: Each new data point traverses the CF-tree from the root, choosing the child whose centroid is closest, until reaching a leaf. If the radius constraint $T$ is violated, a new CF entry is created.

### 4.4 DBSCAN ‚ÄĒ Point Classification Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Input Point p] --> Q{Is N_epsilon p size >= MinPts?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Core Point: Start or Expand Cluster]
    Q -->|No| S{Is p within epsilon of any Core Point?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Border Point: Add to Existing Cluster]
    S -->|No| U[Noise Point: Mark as Outlier]
    R --> V[Cluster A]
    T --> V
    R --> W[Cluster B]
    T --> W
    U --> X[Outlier Set]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Algorithm Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Clustering Algorithms] --> B[Partitioning]
    A --> C[Hierarchical]
    A --> D[Density-Based]
    B --> B1[K-Means: K fixed spherical]
    C --> C1[Agglomerative: Bottom-Up]
    C --> C2[Divisive: Top-Down]
    C --> C3[BIRCH: CF-Tree for large data]
    D --> D1[DBSCAN: Epsilon and MinPts]
    B1 --> E[Pros: Simple fast O N K T]
    C1 --> F[Pros: No K needed dendrogram]
    D1 --> G[Pros: Arbitrary shape outlier detection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: Define clustering. Differentiate between partitioning and hierarchical clustering methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Clustering is an unsupervised learning technique that groups similar data points into clusters such that intra-cluster similarity is maximized and inter-cluster similarity is minimized.

**Partitioning vs. Hierarchical (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Partitioning | Hierarchical |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Structure | Flat, single partition | Nested tree of clusters (dendrogram) |
| Number of clusters $K$ | Must be specified in advance | Can be obtained by cutting dendrogram |
| Flexibility | Less flexible | More flexible, multi-level view |
| Example | K-Means | Agglomerative, BIRCH |

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024]
**Q: Explain the role of the parameters $\epsilon$ and MinPts in DBSCAN.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **$\epsilon$ (Epsilon)** (1 mark): Defines the radius of the neighborhood around each point. It determines the spatial extent within which points are considered "close" to each other. A larger $\epsilon$ merges more points into the same cluster, while a smaller $\epsilon$ creates more isolated clusters.
- **MinPts** (1 mark): The minimum number of points (including the point itself) required inside the $\epsilon$-neighborhood for a point to be classified as a **core point**. Increasing MinPts makes the algorithm more conservative in forming dense regions.
- **Joint Effect** (1 mark): The combination of $\epsilon$ and MinPts defines the density threshold. Tuning them properly is critical ‚ÄĒ too small values produce many noise points, too large values merge distinct clusters.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]

**Q: (a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm in detail. State its objective function and describe the iterative procedure. Mention any **two limitations** of K-Means. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **BIRCH** algorithm? Explain the concept of a **Clustering Feature (CF) vector** and its additive property. Describe the **two-phase procedure** of BIRCH. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) K-Means Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Input**: Dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$, number of clusters $K$.
2. **Initialize**: Choose $K$ initial centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_K$ (randomly or via K-Means++).
3. **Repeat until convergence**:
   - **Assignment Step**: For each $x_i$, assign it to the cluster with the closest centroid:
   $$C(x_i) = \arg\min_{k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$$
   - **Update Step**: Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points:
   $$\mu_k = \frac{1}{|C_k|} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$
4. **Stop** when centroids no longer change.

**Objective Function (2 marks):**

K-Means minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$$

**Two Limitations (1 mark):**

1. **Sensitivity to initialization**: Random initialization can lead to different local minima.
2. **Assumes spherical, equal-sized clusters**: Fails on non-convex, elongated, or arbitrarily shaped clusters.
3. **Requires pre-specifying $K$**: The optimal number of clusters must be known or estimated.
4. **Sensitive to outliers**: Outliers heavily skew the centroid position.

---

**(b) BIRCH Algorithm (7 marks):**

**BIRCH Definition (2 marks):**

BIRCH (**Balanced Iterative Reducing and Clustering using Hierarchies**) is a clustering algorithm designed for **very large datasets**. It builds a **CF-tree** incrementally in a single pass, making it memory-efficient and suitable for streaming data.

**Clustering Feature (CF) Vector (2 marks):**

A CF vector summarizes a subcluster as a 3-tuple: $\text{CF} = (N, \text{LS}, \text{SS})$ where:
- $N$: number of points
- $\text{LS} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ (linear sum)
- $\text{SS} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2$ (squared sum)

**Additive Property** (1 mark): For two disjoint subclusters $A$ and $B$:
$$\text{CF}_{A \cup B} = (N_A + N_B, \text{LS}_A + \text{LS}_B, \text{SS}_A + \text{SS}_B)$$

This allows efficient merging without storing individual points.

**Two-Phase Procedure (2 marks):**

- **Phase 1 (CF-Tree Construction)**: Scan the data once and incrementally insert each point into the tree, following the closest path. The leaf is split if the radius exceeds threshold $T$. A single data point is treated as a CF with $N=1$.
- **Phase 2 (Global Clustering)**: After the tree is built, apply a global clustering algorithm (e.g., K-Means or agglomerative) on the CF entries in the leaves to produce the final clusters. Optional Phase 3 refines the result by redistributing data points.

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]

**Q: (a)** Compare **Agglomerative** and **Divisive** hierarchical clustering. Explain **single, complete, and average linkage** methods with suitable formulas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **DBSCAN**? Explain the concepts of **core point, border point, and noise point** with diagrams. Discuss **two advantages** of DBSCAN over K-Means. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Agglomerative vs. Divisive Hierarchical Clustering (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Agglomerative | Divisive |
|--------|---------------|----------|
| Direction | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| Initial state | $N$ singleton clusters | One cluster with all $N$ points |
| Operation | Merge two closest clusters at each step | Split the least cohesive cluster |
| Time complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ naive, $\mathcal{O}(N^2 \log N)$ with heap | $\mathcal{O}(2^N)$ exponential |
| Practical usage | Common | Rare due to cost |
| Output | Dendrogram | Dendrogram |

**Linkage Methods (4 marks):**

Given two clusters $A$ and $B$ with $n_A$ and $n_B$ points:

- **Single Linkage (1.5 marks)**: Uses the minimum pairwise distance ‚ÄĒ tends to produce long, "chained" clusters.
$$d(A, B) = \min_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a, b)$$

- **Complete Linkage (1.5 marks)**: Uses the maximum pairwise distance ‚ÄĒ produces compact, spherical clusters.
$$d(A, B) = \max_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a, b)$$

- **Average Linkage (1 mark)**: Uses the average pairwise distance ‚ÄĒ a compromise between single and complete.
$$d(A, B) = \frac{1}{n_A n_B} \sum_{a \in A, b \in B} d(a, b)$$

---

**(b) DBSCAN Algorithm (7 marks):**

**DBSCAN Definition (1 mark):**

DBSCAN (**Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise**) is a density-based clustering algorithm that groups together points packed densely and marks points in low-density regions as **outliers**. It does not require specifying the number of clusters in advance.

**Core, Border, and Noise Points (4 marks):**

Let $N_\epsilon(p) = \{q \in \mathcal{D} : d(p, q) \leq \epsilon\}$ denote the $\epsilon$-neighborhood of $p$.

- **Core Point**: A point $p$ is a core point if it has at least MinPts points in its $\epsilon$-neighborhood.
$$|N_\epsilon(p)| \geq \text{MinPts}$$

- **Border Point**: A point $p$ is a border point if it is not a core point, but lies within the $\epsilon$-neighborhood of some core point.
$$|N_\epsilon(p)| < \text{MinPts} \quad \text{and} \quad \exists \, q \in N_\epsilon(p) \text{ such that } q \text{ is core}$$

- **Noise Point (Outlier)**: A point that is neither a core point nor a border point.

**Diagram (1 mark) ‚ÄĒ See Section 4.4 for the schematic flowchart** classifying points as core, border, or noise.

**Two Advantages of DBSCAN over K-Means (1 mark):**

1. **No need to pre-specify $K$**: The number of clusters is determined automatically by the data.
2. **Can find arbitrarily shaped clusters**: Unlike K-Means, which assumes spherical clusters.
3. **Robust to outliers**: Noise points are explicitly identified and not forced into any cluster.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Clustering Questions:**
>
> 1. **K-Means**: Forgetting to mention that the algorithm converges to a **local minimum**, not the global optimum. Always state the **convergence criterion** (centroid change or max iterations).
> 2. **Linkage Criteria**: Students often confuse **single linkage** (minimum) with **complete linkage** (maximum). Memorize: *Single = Smallest distance = Chains; Complete = Largest = Compact.*
> 3. **BIRCH**: Many students write "CF tree" without explaining the **CF vector components** ($N$, LS, SS) and the **additive property**. Both are mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **DBSCAN**: The distinction between **direct density-reachability** and **density-reachability** is frequently tested. Direct reachability is **asymmetric** (core ‚Üí border); density-connectivity is **symmetric**.
> 5. **Hierarchical Clustering**: Forgetting to mention the **time complexity** and stating only "merges clusters" without explaining the **dendrogram** cuts to obtain $K$ clusters.
> 6. **Common Formula Mistake**: Using $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ in the table row using `|` characters breaks the markdown table. Always use $\Vert$ or `\|` LaTeX delimiters.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Clustering Algorithms**

#### **K-Means**
- Partitions data into $K$ spherical clusters; minimizes **WCSS**.
- Iterative: **Assign ‚Üí Update ‚Üí Check convergence**.
- Requires pre-specified $K$; sensitive to **initialization**, **outliers**, and **scaling**.
- Time complexity: $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot K \cdot T \cdot d)$, where $T$ = iterations.

#### **Hierarchical Clustering**
- Produces a **dendrogram** ‚ÄĒ a tree of nested clusters.
- **Agglomerative**: Bottom-up, $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ naive.
- **Divisive**: Top-down, $\mathcal{O}(2^N)$ ‚ÄĒ usually infeasible.
- **Linkage**: Single (min, chains), Complete (max, compact), Average (mean, balanced).
- Does **not** require specifying $K$ in advance ‚ÄĒ cut dendrogram at chosen height.

#### **BIRCH**
- Designed for **large-scale / streaming** datasets; **single-pass**.
- Core data structure: **CF-tree** with CF vectors $(N, \text{LS}, \text{SS})$.
- CF vectors are **additive** ‚ÄĒ efficient merging without raw data.
- Parameters: $B$ (branching factor), $T$ (radius threshold), $L$ (leaf size).
- Two main phases: **CF-tree construction** + **Global clustering** on leaves.

#### **DBSCAN**
- **Density-based**: clusters are dense regions separated by sparse noise.
- Two parameters: **$\epsilon$** (neighborhood radius) and **MinPts** (density threshold).
- Three point types: **Core**, **Border**, **Noise**.
- Properties: **arbitrarily shaped clusters**, **outlier detection**, **no need to pre-specify $K$**.
- Limitations: struggles with **varying densities**; sensitive to parameter tuning.

#### **Comparison Summary**
| Algorithm | Needs $K$? | Cluster Shape | Handles Outliers? | Scalability |
|-----------|------------|---------------|-------------------|-------------|
| K-Means | Yes | Spherical | No | High |
| Agglomerative | No | Arbitrary | No | Low |
| BIRCH | Optional | Spherical-ish | Limited | Very High |
| DBSCAN | No | Arbitrary | Yes (explicit) | Moderate |

#### **Distance Metrics** (Essential)
- **Euclidean**: $L_2$ norm ‚ÄĒ default for K-Means and DBSCAN.
- **Manhattan**: $L_1$ norm ‚ÄĒ robust to outliers.
- **Minkowski**: Generalized form with parameter $p$.
- **Cosine Similarity**: For high-dimensional sparse data (text).

#### **Key KTU Buzzwords to Memorize**
- WCSS / Inertia, K-Means++, Elbow Method, Silhouette Score
- Dendrogram, Proximity Matrix, Lance-Williams Formula
- CF-Tree, Branching Factor, Radius Threshold
- Density-Reachability, Density-Connectivity, $\epsilon$-neighborhood
- Local vs. Global Optimum, Convergence Criterion
