<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Information Retrieval (IR) ‚ÄĒ The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Information Retrieval (IR)* is the science of searching for information in documents, searching for documents themselves, searching for metadata which describes documents, or searching within databases, whether relational databases or unstructured textual corpora. It is concerned with the representation, storage, organization, and access to information items.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Context:** This module builds the *algorithmic and probabilistic foundations* of modern search engines, spam filters, sentiment analyzers, and document classifiers. Mastering this module means understanding the math and logic that Google Search, Gmail spam filtering, and chatbot intent recognition all use internally.

---

## 1.2 Topic-by-Topic Intuitive Overview

### 1.2.1 Boolean Retrieval Model
**Formal Definition:** A classical IR model where documents are retrieved if they satisfy a Boolean expression composed of terms joined by logical operators `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a library where the librarian has a checklist for every book. You say: *"Give me all books about 'cats' AND 'dogs' but NOT 'fish'."* The librarian goes through every book and checks the boxes. That's Boolean Retrieval ‚ÄĒ it's a *binary match* (relevant = 1, not relevant = 0). There is no ranking; a document is either in the result set or it isn't.

> [!TIP]
> **Why we still study it:** Modern search engines use Boolean logic as the *first filter layer* before applying ranking models (like BM25 or cosine similarity).

---

### 1.2.2 Inverted Index
**Formal Definition:** A *data structure* that maps each unique term (word) occurring in a document collection to a *posting list* ‚ÄĒ the list of documents in which that term appears, often with positional or frequency information.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of the index at the **back of a textbook**. Instead of flipping through every page to find "Newton's Laws," you go to the index, look up "Newton's Laws," and it tells you: *"Pages 42, 89, 137."* The **inverted index** is exactly that ‚ÄĒ but for an entire digital library. It "inverts" the natural relationship: *"documents ‚Üí words"* becomes *"words ‚Üí documents."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The standard posting list entry is `(docID, term_frequency)` or `(docID, position)`. In modern systems, the index size is typically **10‚Äď15% of the raw text corpus size**.

---

### 1.2.3 Processing Boolean Queries
**Formal Definition:** The algorithmic procedure by which an IR system evaluates a user's Boolean query against the inverted index, typically using set operations on sorted posting lists.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Like a *Venn diagram calculator*. Given sorted lists `[2, 5, 7, 9]` and `[3, 5, 9, 11]`, finding the `AND` is like finding the intersection of two sorted lists ‚ÄĒ a classic *merge* operation.

---

### 1.2.4 Tokenization
**Formal Definition:** The process of splitting a raw text stream into individual *tokens* (words, numbers, punctuation) ‚ÄĒ the atomic units of indexing.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Like cutting a long sausage into small pieces (tokens) before you can store them in jars. The "sausage" is the document; the "jars" are the index entries.

---

### 1.2.5 Stemming & Lemmatization
**Formal Definition:** *Stemming* is a crude heuristic process that chops off word endings to reduce words to a common "stem." *Lemmatization* is a more refined linguistic process that uses vocabulary and morphological analysis to return the dictionary form (lemma).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine every word is a *tree*. Stemming is like chopping off the branches to get a stump (`connect`, `connected`, `connecting`, `connection` ‚Üí `connect`). Lemmatization is like identifying the *root* of the tree ‚ÄĒ knowing "better" comes from "good."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Algorithm:** The **Porter Stemmer (1980)** is the most widely taught stemming algorithm in KTU syllabi. It applies a cascade of suffix-stripping rules.

---

### 1.2.6 Phrase Queries
**Formal Definition:** A query that treats a sequence of words as a *single semantic unit* (e.g., "machine learning"), requiring the system to retrieve documents where these words appear consecutively (or near each other) in that order.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Searching for "New York" is *not* the same as searching for "York" in a document about *new* products. The phrase query tells the system: *"I need these words to be neighbors."*

---

### 1.2.7 Vector Space Model (VSM)
**Formal Definition:** Documents and queries are represented as *vectors* in a high-dimensional space (one dimension per unique term). Relevance is measured by *similarity* between query and document vectors ‚ÄĒ typically using **cosine similarity**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Every document is a *point in space*. Words are the *axes*. Two documents are "similar" if they point in roughly the same direction. The angle between the vectors tells you how similar they are ‚ÄĒ the smaller the angle, the closer the meaning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formula:** The similarity is given by `cos(őł) = (A¬∑B) / (||A|| ||B||)`, yielding values in `[-1, 1]`. For non-negative TF-IDF vectors, the range is `[0, 1]`.

---

### 1.2.8 Finite Automata and Language Models
**Formal Definition:** A *Finite State Automaton (FSA)* is a computational model with a finite number of states, transitions on input symbols, used here to model linguistic patterns, tokenization rules, and (in some systems) morphological analysis. A *Statistical Language Model* assigns a probability to sequences of words.

**Conceptual Analogy:** An FSA is like a *turnstile at a metro station* ‚ÄĒ it only opens when you insert the right coin in the right order. Insert a nickel, then a dime ‚ÄĒ *open*. Insert a dime, then a nickel ‚ÄĒ *stuck*. The states are the "moods" of the machine; the symbols are the inputs.

---

### 1.2.9 Query Likelihood Model
**Formal Definition:** A probabilistic retrieval model that ranks documents by `P(D | Q)` ‚ÄĒ the probability that the user's query would be *generated by* the document's language model. Using Bayes' rule and a uniform prior on documents, this reduces to ranking by `P(Q | D)`.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine each document is a *person* speaking in a particular style. You overhear a phrase Q. You ask: *"Which speaker is most likely to have said that?"* The person whose speaking style best matches your phrase is the most relevant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Workhorse Formula:** `P(Q | D) = ‚ąŹ P(w_i | D)` for query terms `w_1, w_2, ..., w_n`, with **smoothing** (Jelinek-Mercer or Dirichlet) to handle zero probabilities.

---

### 1.2.10 Na√Įve Bayes Text Classification
**Formal Definition:** A probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem with the *na√Įve* assumption of conditional independence between features (words). For a document `d` with words `w_1...w_n` and class `c`:
`P(c | d) ‚ąĚ P(c) ¬∑ ‚ąŹ P(w_i | c)`.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A *spam filter* that, after seeing thousands of emails, has learned that the word "FREE" appears in 80% of spam but only 5% of normal mail. When a new email arrives, the filter multiplies these little probabilities together and decides: *"Looks more like spam."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Boolean Retrieval Model ‚ÄĒ Operational Logic

The Boolean model treats retrieval as a *set-theoretic* operation:

1. Each document `D_i` is a *set of terms*: `D_i = {t_1, t_2, ..., t_k}`.
2. A query `Q` is a Boolean expression: `Q = t_1 AND (t_2 OR NOT t_3)`.
3. The result is the set of documents for which the Boolean expression evaluates to **TRUE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why and How:** Boolean retrieval is **fast** (uses set intersection/union on sorted posting lists) and **predictable**, but it has a critical flaw: it cannot rank documents. A document with 1 occurrence of a term is treated identically to one with 100 occurrences. This led to the development of ranked retrieval models (VSM, BM25, Language Models).

---

## 2.2 Inverted Index Construction ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Phases
1. **Tokenization & Normalization:** Convert raw text into a stream of normalized tokens.
2. **Linguistic preprocessing:** Apply stemming, lemmatization, case-folding, stop-word removal.
3. **Indexing:** Build a mapping `term ‚Üí posting list`.

### 2.2.2 The Inverted Index Data Structure
- **Dictionary (Lexicon):** Stores all unique terms with their document frequency `df_t` (= number of docs containing `t`).
- **Postings File:** For each term, a sorted list of `(docID, term_frequency[, positions])`.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Posting lists are kept *sorted by `docID`* to enable **merge-join** style processing of Boolean queries in `O(|L1| + |L2|)` time.

---

## 2.3 Processing Boolean Queries ‚ÄĒ Algorithm

**Query:** `Q = A AND B`
**Input:** Posting lists `L_A` and `L_B`, both sorted by `docID`.
**Algorithm:** Standard *merge intersection* (like the merge step of merge-sort).

```
i = 0; j = 0
while i < len(L_A) and j < len(L_B):
    if L_A[i] == L_B[j]:    output L_A[i]; i++; j++
    elif L_A[i] < L_B[j]:   i++
    else:                   j++
```

For `OR`, replace `==` with output; for `AND NOT`, skip the `j++` on match.

---

## 2.4 Tokenization ‚ÄĒ Why It Is Non-Trivial

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students think tokenization is "just split on spaces." In reality, it involves:
> - Handling hyphens (`co-education` ‚Üí `co`, `education` OR keep as one)
> - Handling apostrophes (`don't` ‚Üí `don`, `t` OR `do`, `not`)
> - Handling punctuation (`.`, `,`, `;` ‚ÄĒ strip them)
> - Handling special tokens (emails, URLs, numbers, dates)
> - **Unicode normalization** for non-English scripts

---

## 2.5 Stemming ‚ÄĒ Porter Stemmer Highlights

The Porter Stemmer applies **5 phases of suffix-stripping rules**, each phase transforming the word using measurable conditions (vowel count, measure `m`, suffix patterns).

**Examples of rules (Phase 1a, 1b):**
- `sses ‚Üí ss` (caresses ‚Üí caress)
- `ies ‚Üí i` (ponies ‚Üí poni)
- `ss ‚Üí ss` (caress ‚Üí caress)
- `s ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` (cats ‚Üí cat)
- `ing ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` (walking ‚Üí walk) if stem contains a vowel
- `ed ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` (plastered ‚Üí plaster) if stem contains a vowel

> [!NOTE]
> **The Porter Stemmer does NOT use a dictionary.** It is purely rule-based. This makes it fast and language-agnostic for simple cases, but occasionally over-stems (e.g., `university` ‚Üí `univers`).

---

## 2.6 Phrase Queries ‚ÄĒ Biword & Positional Index

Two standard approaches:

1. **Biword Index:** Index every *consecutive pair* of terms. Query "machine learning" looks up the biword `"machine learning"` in the dictionary. **Problem:** Explodes in size and cannot handle queries of length > 2.

2. **Positional Index:** Each posting list stores not only `(docID, tf)` but also a list of *positions* where the term occurs. To find the phrase, intersect the posting lists and check for consecutive position numbers.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Approach:** Positional index is the industry default. It costs roughly **~35% extra storage** over a non-positional index but enables arbitrary phrase queries.

---

## 2.7 Vector Space Model ‚ÄĒ Detailed Mechanics

### 2.7.1 Term Weighting
The two most critical weights are:

1. **Term Frequency (TF):** `tf(t, d)` = number of times term `t` appears in document `d`.
2. **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** Measures how "rare" a term is across the collection.

$$\text{idf}(t) = \log \frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$$

where `N` = total number of documents, `df(t)` = number of documents containing term `t`.

The combined **TF-IDF weight** is:

$$w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t, d) \cdot \log \frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$$

### 2.7.2 Cosine Similarity
For a query vector $\vec{q}$ and a document vector $\vec{d}$:

$$\text{cos}(\theta) = \frac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\|\vec{q}\| \, \|\vec{d}\|} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{V} w_{i,q} \, w_{i,d}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{V} w_{i,q}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{V} w_{i,d}^2}}$$

Documents are ranked by descending `cos(őł)`.

---

## 2.8 Finite Automata in IR

A **Finite Automaton** `M = (Q, ő£, őī, q_0, F)` consists of:
- `Q` = finite set of states
- `ő£` = input alphabet
- `őī : Q √ó ő£ ‚Üí Q` = transition function
- `q_0` = start state
- `F ‚äÜ Q` = set of accepting states

**Use cases in IR:**
- Tokenization rules
- Pattern matching (regex engines are implemented as FSAs)
- Morphological analysis (some stemmers are FSA-based)
- N-gram generation

---

## 2.9 Query Likelihood Model ‚ÄĒ Probabilistic Foundation

### 2.9.1 Bayes' Rule Application
Rank documents by `P(D | Q)`. By Bayes' rule:

$$P(D \mid Q) = \frac{P(Q \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(Q)}$$

Assuming a uniform prior `P(D) = 1/N`, ranking by `P(D | Q)` is equivalent to ranking by `P(Q | D)`.

### 2.9.2 The Unigram Assumption
Assuming query terms are conditionally independent:

$$P(Q \mid D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid D)$$

### 2.9.3 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
The simplest estimate is:

$$P_{\text{MLE}}(w \mid D) = \frac{\text{tf}(w, D)}{|D|}$$

where `|D|` is the total word count in `D`.

### 2.9.4 Smoothing ‚ÄĒ The Critical Need
`P_MLE(w | D) = 0` for any word not in `D`, which makes the product zero. **Smoothing** redistributes probability mass. The two most common are:

**(a) Jelinek-Mercer Smoothing:**

$$P(w \mid D) = \lambda \cdot P_{\text{MLE}}(w \mid D) + (1 - \lambda) \cdot P_{\text{MLE}}(w \mid C)$$

where `C` is the *collection* language model and `őĽ ‚ąą [0, 1]` (typically `0.7`).

**(b) Dirichlet Prior Smoothing:**

$$P(w \mid D) = \frac{\text{tf}(w, D) + \mu \cdot P_{\text{MLE}}(w \mid C)}{|D| + \mu}$$

where `őľ` is a smoothing parameter (typically `2000` for short queries).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without smoothing, the Query Likelihood Model collapses** ‚ÄĒ any document missing even a single query term gets a score of 0, regardless of how relevant it is otherwise.

---

## 2.10 Na√Įve Bayes Text Classification ‚ÄĒ Detailed Theory

### 2.10.1 Bayes' Theorem
For a document `d` and class `c ‚ąą {c_1, c_2, ..., c_k}`:

$$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(d \mid c)}{P(d)}$$

Since `P(d)` is constant across classes, classification reduces to:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot P(d \mid c)$$

### 2.10.2 The Na√Įve Assumption
Assume all features (words) are conditionally independent given the class:

$$P(d \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

### 2.10.3 Final Decision Rule

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

### 2.10.4 MLE Estimates with Laplace Smoothing
To avoid zero probabilities:

$$P(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w, c) + 1}{\sum_{w' \in V} \text{count}(w', c) + \vert V \vert}$$

$$P(c) = \frac{\text{count}(c)}{N}$$

where `|V|` is the vocabulary size.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Na√Įve"?** The independence assumption is almost always violated in real text (e.g., "ice" and "cream" are correlated). But empirically, Na√Įve Bayes performs surprisingly well ‚ÄĒ especially for *spam filtering* and *sentiment analysis* ‚ÄĒ because the ranking is often preserved even when probabilities are inaccurate.

---

## 2.11 KTU 2024 Scheme ‚ÄĒ High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Document Frequency** | `df(t) =` number of docs containing term `t` | Integer ‚Č• 0 |
| 2 | **Collection Frequency** | `cf(t) =` total occurrences of `t` in collection | Integer ‚Č• 0 |
| 3 | **Inverse Document Frequency** | $\text{idf}(t) = \log(N / \text{df}(t))$ | Log base usually `e` or `10` |
| 4 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \text{idf}(t)$ | Real number ‚Č• 0 |
| 5 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(q, d) = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\Vert \vec{q} \Vert \, \Vert \vec{d} \Vert}$ | Range `[0, 1]` for non-negative vectors |
| 6 | **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(c \mid d) = P(c) P(d \mid c) / P(d)$ | Probabilities in `[0, 1]` |
| 7 | **Na√Įve Bayes Decision** | $c^* = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid c)$ | Sum in log-space for numerical stability |
| 8 | **Na√Įve Bayes (Laplace)** | $P(w \mid c) = [\text{count}(w,c) + 1] / [\sum_{w'} \text{count}(w',c) + \vert V \vert]$ | `|V|` = vocab size |
| 9 | **Query Likelihood (MLE)** | $P(w \mid D) = \text{tf}(w, D) / \vert D \vert$ | `\|D\|` = total words in `D` |
| 10 | **Jelinek-Mercer Smoothing** | $P(w \mid D) = \lambda P_{\text{MLE}}(w \mid D) + (1-\lambda) P_{\text{MLE}}(w \mid C)$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$, typically `0.7` |
| 11 | **Dirichlet Smoothing** | $P(w \mid D) = [\text{tf}(w,D) + \mu P(w \mid C)] / [\vert D \vert + \mu]$ | $\mu \approx 2000$ for short queries |
| 12 | **Porter Stemmer** | Rule-based suffix stripping, 5 phases | No dictionary required |
| 13 | **Biword Index** | Index all consecutive term pairs | Storage cost $\approx \sum_i (\vert D_i \vert - 1)$ |
| 14 | **Positional Index Storage Overhead** | $\sim 35\%$ above non-positional | Industry standard |
| 15 | **Merge Intersection Cost** | $O(\vert L_1 \vert + \vert L_2 \vert)$ | Both lists must be sorted |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In numerical questions, *always show units and intermediate steps*. The formula table above is the **minimum** you should be able to reproduce from memory.

---

## 2.12 Real-World Utility of These Models

| Model | Real-World Application |
|-------|------------------------|
| Boolean Retrieval | Legal document databases (Westlaw, LexisNexis), patent search |
| Inverted Index | **Elasticsearch, Apache Lucene, Solr, Google Search** (all use it) |
| Vector Space Model | Early web search engines (SMART system), document clustering |
| Query Likelihood Model | Modern web search ranking (with smoothing), speech recognition |
| Na√Įve Bayes | **Spam filtering (Gmail), Sentiment Analysis (Twitter), Medical Diagnosis** |
| Finite Automata | Regex matching, spell-checkers, morphological analyzers, **lexical analyzers in compilers** |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ‚ÄĒ Inverted Index Construction

### 3.1.1 The Document Collection
Let us work with a canonical KTU textbook example (Manning, Raghavan, Sch√ľtze style, but used here as a *pedagogical* sample, not as a cited reference):

```
D1: "I love data analytics and machine learning"
D2: "Data science is the future of analytics"
D3: "Machine learning drives modern analytics"
D4: "I love deep learning and neural networks"
```

### 3.1.2 Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Tokenization & Normalization
Convert each document to lowercase, split on whitespace and punctuation, remove stop words (`I`, `is`, `the`, `of`, `and`) and apply minimal stemming:

| Doc | Tokens (after preprocessing) |
|-----|------------------------------|
| D1  | `love, data, analyt, machin, learn` |
| D2  | `data, scienc, futur, analyt` |
| D3  | `machin, learn, drive, modern, analyt` |
| D4  | `love, deep, learn, neural, network` |

### 3.1.3 Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Build Term-to-Document Mapping
For each term, list the documents in which it appears, sorted by `docID`:

```
analyt:   D1 ‚Üí D2 ‚Üí D3
data:     D1 ‚Üí D2
deep:     D4
drive:    D3
futur:    D2
learn:    D1 ‚Üí D3 ‚Üí D4
love:     D1 ‚Üí D4
machin:   D1 ‚Üí D3
modern:   D3
network:  D4
neural:   D4
scienc:   D2
```

### 3.1.4 Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Add Term Frequencies (TF) to Postings
```
analyt:   (D1, 1), (D2, 1), (D3, 1)
data:     (D1, 1), (D2, 1)
deep:     (D4, 1)
drive:    (D3, 1)
futur:    (D2, 1)
learn:    (D1, 1), (D3, 1), (D4, 1)
love:     (D1, 1), (D4, 1)
machin:   (D1, 1), (D3, 1)
modern:   (D3, 1)
network:  (D4, 1)
neural:   (D4, 1)
scienc:   (D2, 1)
```

### 3.1.5 Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compute Document Frequencies
`df(analyt) = 3`, `df(learn) = 3`, `df(love) = 2`, `df(data) = 2`, `df(machin) = 2`, others = 1. Total `N = 4`.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Compute IDF (using natural log)
Using `idf(t) = ln(N / df(t))`:

$$\text{idf}(\text{analyt}) = \ln(4/3) = \ln(1.333) \approx 0.2877$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{learn}) = \ln(4/3) \approx 0.2877$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{love}) = \ln(4/2) = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{data}) = \ln(4/2) \approx 0.6931$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{machin}) = \ln(4/2) \approx 0.6931$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{deep}) = \ln(4/1) = \ln(4) \approx 1.3863$$

The same value `1.3863` applies to all terms with `df = 1`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** A term appearing in *only one* document (like `deep`) gets a *high* IDF ‚ÄĒ it is a "rare" and therefore more *discriminative* term. A term appearing in *three out of four* documents (like `analyt`) gets a *low* IDF ‚ÄĒ it is too common to distinguish documents.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ‚ÄĒ Boolean Query Processing via Merge

### 3.2.1 Query
$$Q = \text{``machine''} \text{ AND } \text{``learning''}$$

### 3.2.2 Posting Lists (sorted by docID)
```
L_machin = [D1, D3]
L_learn  = [D1, D3, D4]
```

### 3.2.3 Merge-Intersection Algorithm Trace

| Step | i (L_machin) | j (L_learn) | L_machin[i] | L_learn[j] | Action | Output |
|------|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------|--------|
| 0    | 0            | 0            | D1           | D1           | Equal  | D1     |
| 1    | 1            | 1            | D3           | D3           | Equal  | D3     |
| 2    | 2            | 2            | ‚ÄĒ (end)      | D4           | Stop (i exhausted) | ‚ÄĒ |

**Result:** `{D1, D3}`

> [!TIP]
> **Time Complexity:** `O(|L_machin| + |L_learn|) = O(2 + 3) = O(5)`. For an `OR` query, we output on `<=`, `<`, `==` and advance whichever pointer is smaller. For `AND NOT`, we output from the first list whenever the current `docID` is *not* in the second list.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 ‚ÄĒ Vector Space Model Cosine Similarity

### 3.3.1 Setup
- Vocabulary (3 terms for simplicity): `V = {analytics, machine, learning}`
- `N = 4` documents
- Using TF-IDF weights computed above (assuming `tf = 1` for all occurrences)

### 3.3.2 Document Vectors
Since `analyt` and `learn` have `df = 3` and `machin` has `df = 2`:

$$\text{idf}_{\text{analyt}} = \text{idf}_{\text{learn}} \approx 0.2877$$

$$\text{idf}_{\text{machin}} \approx 0.6931$$

So the term weights are approximately:
- `analytics`: `0.2877`
- `learning`: `0.2877`
- `machine`: `0.6931`

### 3.3.3 Document Vectors
- D1 ("love data **analytics** **machine** **learning**"):
$$\vec{d_1} = (0.2877,\; 0.6931,\; 0.2877)$$

- D3 ("**machine** **learning** drives modern **analytics**"):
$$\vec{d_3} = (0.2877,\; 0.6931,\; 0.2877)$$

- D4 ("love deep **learning** and neural networks"):
$$\vec{d_4} = (0,\; 0,\; 0.2877)$$

### 3.3.4 Query Vector
For query `Q = "machine learning"` (only `machin` and `learn` have non-zero weights):
$$\vec{q} = (0,\; 0.6931,\; 0.2877)$$

### 3.3.5 Cosine Similarity Computation: sim(Q, D1)

$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_1} = (0)(0.2877) + (0.6931)(0.6931) + (0.2877)(0.2877)$$

$$= 0 + 0.4804 + 0.0828 = 0.5632$$

$$\|\vec{q}\| = \sqrt{0^2 + 0.6931^2 + 0.2877^2} = \sqrt{0.4804 + 0.0828} = \sqrt{0.5632} \approx 0.7505$$

$$\|\vec{d_1}\| = \sqrt{0.2877^2 + 0.6931^2 + 0.2877^2} = \sqrt{0.0828 + 0.4804 + 0.0828} = \sqrt{0.6460} \approx 0.8037$$

$$\text{sim}(Q, D_1) = \frac{0.5632}{0.7505 \times 0.8037} = \frac{0.5632}{0.6032} \approx 0.9338$$

### 3.3.6 Cosine Similarity Computation: sim(Q, D3)
By symmetry of D1 and D3 vectors: `sim(Q, D3) ‚Čą 0.9338`.

### 3.3.7 Cosine Similarity Computation: sim(Q, D4)

$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_4} = (0)(0) + (0.6931)(0) + (0.2877)(0.2877) = 0.0828$$

$$\|\vec{d_4}\| = \sqrt{0^2 + 0^2 + 0.2877^2} = 0.2877$$

$$\text{sim}(Q, D_4) = \frac{0.0828}{0.7505 \times 0.2877} = \frac{0.0828}{0.2159} \approx 0.3835$$

### 3.3.8 Final Ranking

| Rank | Document | Cosine Similarity |
|------|----------|-------------------|
| 1    | D1       | 0.9338            |
| 1    | D3       | 0.9338            |
| 3    | D4       | 0.3835            |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** D1 and D3 both contain all three terms with similar weights, so they are tied. D4 only contains `learning`, so it gets a much lower score despite containing *one* of the query terms. The VSM elegantly handles partial matches.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 ‚ÄĒ Na√Įve Bayes Text Classification

### 3.4.1 Training Data
We have a *toy* spam dataset:

| Document | Text                                          | Class |
|----------|-----------------------------------------------|-------|
| D1       | "cheap meds free"                             | Spam  |
| D2       | "free offer cheap deal"                       | Spam  |
| D3       | "meeting schedule at office"                  | Ham   |
| D4       | "project deadline at office"                  | Ham   |
| D5       | "free holiday offer"                          | Spam  |
| D6       | "schedule meeting with team"                  | Ham   |

### 3.4.2 Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Class Priors
$$P(\text{Spam}) = 3/6 = 0.5$$

$$P(\text{Ham}) = 3/6 = 0.5$$

### 3.4.3 Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Vocabulary & Word Counts per Class
Vocabulary `V = {cheap, meds, free, offer, deal, holiday, meeting, schedule, office, project, deadline, team}` ‚Üí `|V| = 12`.

**Spam word counts:**
- `cheap`: 2, `meds`: 1, `free`: 3, `offer`: 2, `deal`: 1, `holiday`: 1
- Total Spam tokens = 10

**Ham word counts:**
- `meeting`: 2, `schedule`: 2, `office`: 2, `project`: 1, `deadline`: 1, `team`: 1
- Total Ham tokens = 9

### 3.4.4 Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Laplace-Smoothed Conditional Probabilities
Formula: $P(w \mid c) = [\text{count}(w, c) + 1] / [\text{count}_{\text{total}}(c) + |V|]$

**For Spam (denominator = 10 + 12 = 22):**

$$P(\text{cheap} \mid \text{Spam}) = (2+1)/22 = 3/22 \approx 0.1364$$

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{Spam}) = (3+1)/22 = 4/22 \approx 0.1818$$

$$P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Spam}) = (2+1)/22 = 3/22 \approx 0.1364$$

$$P(\text{meeting} \mid \text{Spam}) = (0+1)/22 = 1/22 \approx 0.0455$$

**For Ham (denominator = 9 + 12 = 21):**

$$P(\text{cheap} \mid \text{Ham}) = (0+1)/21 = 1/21 \approx 0.0476$$

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{Ham}) = (0+1)/21 = 1/21 \approx 0.0476$$

$$P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Ham}) = (0+1)/21 = 1/21 \approx 0.0476$$

$$P(\text{meeting} \mid \text{Ham}) = (2+1)/21 = 3/21 \approx 0.1429$$

### 3.4.5 Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Classify New Document
**Test document:** `d = "free cheap offer"`

**Score for Spam (using log-likelihood to avoid underflow):**

$$\log P(\text{Spam} \mid d) \propto \log P(\text{Spam}) + \log P(\text{free} \mid \text{Spam}) + \log P(\text{cheap} \mid \text{Spam}) + \log P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Spam})$$

$$= \log(0.5) + \log(0.1818) + \log(0.1364) + \log(0.1364)$$

$$= -0.6931 + (-1.7047) + (-1.9924) + (-1.9924) = -6.3826$$

**Score for Ham:**

$$\log P(\text{Ham} \mid d) \propto \log P(\text{Ham}) + \log P(\text{free} \mid \text{Ham}) + \log P(\text{cheap} \mid \text{Ham}) + \log P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Ham})$$

$$= \log(0.5) + 3 \times \log(0.0476)$$

$$= -0.6931 + 3 \times (-3.0445) = -0.6931 + (-9.1336) = -9.8267$$

### 3.4.6 Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Decision
Since `-6.3826 > -9.8267`, the document is classified as **SPAM**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why log?** Multiplying many small probabilities leads to *arithmetic underflow* (the product becomes 0 in floating point). Adding logs keeps numbers in a safe range. KTU examiners often look for this trick.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 ‚ÄĒ Query Likelihood Model with Smoothing

### 3.5.1 Setup
- `D = "the analytics analytics machine"` ‚Üí `|D| = 4`
- Collection `C` has 1000 documents, total tokens `|C| = 1,000,000`
- `tf("analytics", D) = 2`, `tf("machine", D) = 1`, `tf("learning", D) = 0`
- Collection MLE: `P("analytics" | C) = 0.0001`, `P("machine" | C) = 0.00005`, `P("learning" | C) = 0.00003`
- Query: `Q = "machine learning"`

### 3.5.2 Without Smoothing (MLE)

$$P(Q \mid D) = P(\text{machine} \mid D) \times P(\text{learning} \mid D)$$

$$= \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{0}{4} = 0.25 \times 0 = 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Problem:** Score is **zero** despite `D` containing `machine`. This is the *zero-probability catastrophe* and the reason smoothing is mandatory.

### 3.5.3 With Jelinek-Mercer Smoothing (`őĽ = 0.7`)

$$P(\text{machine} \mid D) = 0.7 \times \frac{1}{4} + 0.3 \times 0.00005 = 0.175 + 0.000015 = 0.175015$$

$$P(\text{learning} \mid D) = 0.7 \times \frac{0}{4} + 0.3 \times 0.00003 = 0 + 0.000009 = 0.000009$$

$$P(Q \mid D) = 0.175015 \times 0.000009 \approx 1.575 \times 10^{-6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The score is now a small but *non-zero* number. Smoothing rescues the document from a false negative. The term `learning` gets a tiny probability from the collection model, and `machine` retains a high probability from the document.

### 3.5.4 With Dirichlet Smoothing (`őľ = 2000`)

$$P(\text{machine} \mid D) = \frac{1 + 2000 \times 0.00005}{4 + 2000} = \frac{1 + 0.1}{2004} = \frac{1.1}{2004} \approx 0.000549$$

$$P(\text{learning} \mid D) = \frac{0 + 2000 \times 0.00003}{4 + 2000} = \frac{0.06}{2004} \approx 0.000030$$

$$P(Q \mid D) = 0.000549 \times 0.000030 \approx 1.65 \times 10^{-8}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison:** Dirichlet smoothing is *more conservative* for short documents because it adds a large constant `őľ` to the denominator. Jelinek-Mercer is preferred for verbose documents.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 ‚ÄĒ Porter Stemmer Demonstration

Starting word: `connect`

| Phase | Rule Applied | Result | Reason |
|-------|--------------|--------|--------|
| 1a    | `sses ‚Üí ss` (N/A) | `connect` | Does not match |
| 1a    | `ies ‚Üí i` (N/A) | `connect` | Does not match |
| 1b    | `ing ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` (N/A) | `connect` | Does not match |
| 2     | `ational ‚Üí ate` (N/A) | `connect` | Does not match |
| 3     | `icate ‚Üí ic` (N/A) | `connect` | Does not match |
| 4     | `al ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` (N/A) | `connect` | Does not match |
| 5a   | `e ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` (N/A unless measure > 1) | `connect` | Measure = 1, rule skipped |
| 5b   | `ll ‚Üí l` (N/A) | `connect` | Does not match |

**Final stem:** `connect`

Starting word: `connection`

After phase 1a/1b: still `connection` (no matching rule).
After phase 2: rule `(m>0) ion ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` matches because measure(`connect`) = 1 > 0 and suffix is `ion` ‚Üí `connect`.
After phase 5a: `e ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` skipped because stem has measure = 1, not > 1.
**Final stem:** `connect`.

Starting word: `connected`

Phase 1b rule `(m>0) ed ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` applies because measure(`connect`) = 1 > 0 and stem `connect` still has a vowel.
**Final stem:** `connect`.

All three words ‚ÄĒ `connect`, `connection`, `connected` ‚ÄĒ reduce to the same stem `connect`, demonstrating the *stemming goal of conflating morphological variants*.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inverted Index ‚ÄĒ Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Document Collection] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Normalizer<br/>lowercase, strip punctuation]
    C --> D[Stop-Word Filter]
    D --> E[Stemmer<br/>Porter Algorithm]
    E --> F[Indexer]
    F --> G[Dictionary / Lexicon<br/>term: df, offset]
    F --> H[Postings File<br/>docID, tf, positions]
    G -.lookup.-> I[Query Processor]
    H -.scan.-> I
    I --> J[Ranking Engine]
    J --> K[Top-K Results]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The upper path builds the *dictionary*; the lower path writes the *postings file*. At query time, the system looks up query terms in the dictionary and follows pointers to fetch the relevant posting lists.

---

## 4.2 Boolean Query Processing ‚ÄĒ Merge Intersection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0([Start]) --> A1[Initialize pointers i=0, j=0]
    A1 --> A2{i and j within bounds?}
    A2 -- No --> P1([End of algorithm])
    A2 -- Yes --> B1{Compare L_A[i] vs L_B[j]}
    B1 -- Equal --> C1[Output L_A i to result set]
    C1 --> C2[i = i + 1]
    C2 --> C3[j = j + 1]
    C3 --> A2
    B1 -- L_A i less than L_B j --> D1[i = i + 1]
    D1 --> A2
    B1 -- L_A i greater than L_B j --> E1[j = j + 1]
    E1 --> A2
```

---

## 4.3 Vector Space Model ‚ÄĒ Document Query Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Dimension_Space [Vocabulary Dimensions]
        AX[T1 axis<br/>e.g., analytics]
        BX[T2 axis<br/>e.g., machine]
        CX[T3 axis<br/>e.g., learning]
    end
    AX -- coordinate --> P1[Document Vector d1]
    BX -- coordinate --> P1
    CX -- coordinate --> P1
    AX -- coordinate --> P2[Query Vector q]
    BX -- coordinate --> P2
    CX -- coordinate --> P2
    P1 -- cosine theta --> SIM[Similarity Score]
    P2 -- cosine theta --> SIM
    SIM --> RANK[Ranking]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Reading:** The vectors `d1` and `q` emanate from the origin in a 3-D space (one axis per term). The angle `őł` between them is what we minimize (or equivalently, what cosine-similarity maximizes). Smaller `őł` ‚Üí higher similarity.

---

## 4.4 Na√Įve Bayes ‚ÄĒ Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DOC([New Document d]) --> TOK[Tokenize d into words w1..wn]
    TOK --> LOOKUP[Look up P wi given c for each class c]
    LOOKUP --> PRIOR[Multiply by class prior P c]
    PRIOR --> SUMM[Sum log probabilities<br/>for numerical stability]
    SUMM --> ARGMAX[argmax over all classes c]
    ARGMAX --> OUT([Predicted class c star])
```

---

## 4.5 Query Likelihood Model ‚ÄĒ Smoothing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q([Query Q with words w1..wn]) --> CALC[For each document D]
    CALC --> MLE[Compute MLE P wi given D]
    MLE --> MIX[Mix with collection model]
    MIX --> SMOOTH[Apply Jelinek Mercer or Dirichlet]
    SMOOTH --> MULT[Multiply all P wi given D]
    MULT --> RANK[Rank documents by score]
    RANK --> TOPK[Return Top K]
```

---

## 4.6 Finite Automaton ‚ÄĒ Simple Example for Tokenization

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0
    s0 --> s0 : letter or digit
    s0 --> s1 : space
    s0 --> s2 : punctuation
    s1 --> s1 : space
    s1 --> s0 : letter or digit
    s2 --> s0 : letter or digit
    s2 --> s1 : space
    s0 --> [*] : end of input
    s1 --> [*] : end of input
    s2 --> [*] : end of input
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the State Diagram:**
> - `s0` = *Inside a word* state (accepting ‚ÄĒ emit token)
> - `s1` = *Inside whitespace* state (non-accepting)
> - `s2` = *Inside punctuation* state (accepting ‚ÄĒ emit punctuation as separate token)
> This is a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) for simple word and punctuation tokenization.

---

## 4.7 Stemming Pipeline ‚ÄĒ Porter Stemmer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Input word]) --> P1[Phase 1a: plurals and -s endings]
    P1 --> P2[Phase 1b: -ed, -ing past/present participles]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 1c: y to i]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 2: -ational, -tional, -enci etc.]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 3: -icate, -ative, -alize etc.]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 4: -al, -ance, -ence, -er etc.]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 5a: final -e removal]
    P7 --> P8[Phase 5b: final -ll to -l]
    P8 --> O([Output stem])
```

---

## 4.8 Complete Module 4 ‚ÄĒ Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BOOLEAN[Boolean Retrieval] --> INVERTED[Inverted Index]
    INVERTED --> PROCESS[Query Processing]
    TOKEN[Tokenization] --> STEM[Stemming]
    TOKEN --> INVERTED
    STEM --> INVERTED
    PHRASE[Phrase Queries] --> INVERTED
    VSM[Vector Space Model] --> INVERTED
    FSA[Finite Automata] --> TOKEN
    LANG[Language Model] --> QLM[Query Likelihood]
    QLM --> SMOOTH[Smoothing]
    NB[Naive Bayes] --> CLASS[Text Classification]
    INVERTED --> RANKED[Ranked Retrieval]
    VSM --> RANKED
    QLM --> RANKED
    CLASS --> APPS[Spam filtering, sentiment, search ranking]
    RANKED --> APPS
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`
**Define an Inverted Index. How is it different from a forward index? Illustrate with a small example.**

**Model Answer:**

An **inverted index** is a data structure used in Information Retrieval that maps each unique term appearing in a document collection to a list of documents (and optionally positions) in which that term occurs. It consists of two parts: (i) a **dictionary** (or *lexicon*) of all unique terms, and (ii) a **postings file** containing, for each term, a sorted list of document IDs and associated metadata such as term frequency or positional information.

A **forward index**, in contrast, maps each *document* to the list of terms it contains: `D_i ‚Üí [t_1, t_2, ..., t_k]`. The forward index is the *natural* representation of a document, but it is inefficient for query processing because finding all documents containing a term requires scanning every document's list.

**Example:** For the documents
- D1: "data science"
- D2: "machine learning"
- D3: "data mining"

**Forward Index:**
- D1 ‚Üí {data, science}
- D2 ‚Üí {machine, learning}
- D3 ‚Üí {data, mining}

**Inverted Index:**
- data ‚Üí {D1, D3}
- science ‚Üí {D1}
- machine ‚Üí {D2}
- learning ‚Üí {D2}
- mining ‚Üí {D3}

The inverted index is preferred for retrieval because queries can directly look up relevant documents without scanning the entire collection. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Similar Pattern]`
**Explain the concept of stemming. Why is it used in IR? Apply the Porter Stemmer rules to the words "running", "national", and "studies", and write their stems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Stemming** is the process of reducing a word to its root or base form (the *stem*) by chopping off word endings. It is a heuristic, rule-based process that does not require a dictionary. It is used in IR to conflate morphological variants of a word (e.g., *connect*, *connected*, *connection*, *connects*) into a single index term, thereby:
1. Reducing the size of the inverted index.
2. Improving recall, because searching for one stem retrieves documents containing all variants.

**Applying Porter Stemmer rules:**

- **"running"** ‚Üí Phase 1b rule `(m>0) ing ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` applies because the stem `runn` contains a vowel and the measure of `runn` is `m = 1 > 0`. Result: `runn`. Then in Phase 5a, if the stem ends in a single `e` and measure > 1, it is removed ‚ÄĒ but here the measure of `runn` is 1, so the rule does not apply. **Final stem: `runn`** (in practice, Porter outputs `run` after the additional rule that doubles consonants are sometimes simplified; the standard Porter output is **`run`**).

- **"national"** ‚Üí Phase 2 rule `(m>0) ational ‚Üí ate` applies. `nation` has measure `m = 2 > 0`. Result: `nate`. Then Phase 5a rule `(m>1) e ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` does not apply (measure of `nate` is 1). **Final stem: `nate`**.

- **"studies"** ‚Üí Phase 1a rule `(m>0) sses ‚Üí ‚ąÖ, (m>0) ies ‚Üí i, (m>0) ss ‚Üí ‚ąÖ ss` ‚ÄĒ the rule `(m>0) ies ‚Üí i` applies (studi has measure `m = 1 > 0`). Result: `studi`. Then Phase 5a rule `(m>1) e ‚Üí ‚ąÖ` does not apply. **Final stem: `studi`**.

**Stems obtained:** `run`, `nate`, `studi`. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted Pattern]`**
**Construct an inverted index for the following document collection. Show tokenization, normalization, and the final inverted index with term frequencies.**

```
D1: "Information retrieval is the science of search"
D2: "Search engines use information retrieval"
D3: "Science and retrieval are foundational"
D4: "Modern information systems enable search"
```

Also compute the IDF for the term `"search"` and `"retrieval"` (use `log` base 10, take `N = 4`).

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks
**Explain the Vector Space Model. Using the index you built in part (a), represent D1 and the query Q = "search information" as TF-IDF vectors and compute their cosine similarity. State which document among D1, D2, D3, D4 is most similar to Q.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted Pattern]`**
**Explain Bayes' Theorem and the Na√Įve Bayes assumption. Given the small training set below, classify the test document "cheap free offer" as either SPAM or HAM using Na√Įve Bayes text classification with Laplace smoothing.**

| Document | Text                  | Class |
|----------|-----------------------|-------|
| D1       | "cheap free offer"    | SPAM  |
| D2       | "free offer deal"     | SPAM  |
| D3       | "project meeting"     | HAM   |
| D4       | "meeting project"     | HAM   |

Use `|V| = 6` (vocabulary = {cheap, free, offer, deal, project, meeting}).

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks
**Describe the Query Likelihood Model. Why is smoothing essential? Demonstrate with an example (any small corpus) how Jelinek-Mercer smoothing prevents the zero-probability problem.**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions for Question A

### Solution to Question A (a)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Tokenization & Normalization (lowercase, split, no stemming for this problem):**

| Doc | Tokens |
|-----|--------|
| D1  | `information, retrieval, is, the, science, of, search` |
| D2  | `search, engines, use, information, retrieval` |
| D3  | `science, and, retrieval, are, foundational` |
| D4  | `modern, information, systems, enable, search` |

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Inverted Index with Term Frequencies:**

```
information:    (D1, 1), (D2, 1), (D4, 1)
retrieval:      (D1, 1), (D2, 1), (D3, 1)
search:         (D1, 1), (D2, 1), (D4, 1)
science:        (D1, 1), (D3, 1)
engines:        (D2, 1)
use:            (D2, 1)
and:            (D3, 1)
are:            (D3, 1)
foundational:   (D3, 1)
modern:         (D4, 1)
systems:        (D4, 1)
enable:         (D4, 1)
is:             (D1, 1)
the:            (D1, 1)
of:             (D1, 1)
```

*(For brevity, stop words like `is`, `the`, `of`, `and`, `are` are sometimes excluded; the question does not specify, so we keep them.)*

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Document Frequencies:**
- `df(search) = 3` (appears in D1, D2, D4)
- `df(retrieval) = 3` (appears in D1, D2, D3)
- `df(information) = 3`
- `df(science) = 2`
- Others = 1

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ IDF Computation (log base 10, N = 4):**

$$\text{idf}(\text{search}) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(\text{search})}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) = \log_{10}(1.333) \approx 0.1249$$

$$\text{idf}(\text{retrieval}) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{\text{df}(\text{retrieval})}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \approx 0.1249$$

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

### Solution to Question A (b)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Vector Space Model Definition:**

The Vector Space Model represents documents and queries as vectors in a high-dimensional space where each dimension corresponds to a unique term in the vocabulary. The weight of each dimension is typically the TF-IDF value. Relevance is measured by the **cosine similarity** between the query vector and the document vector, which is the cosine of the angle between them.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ TF-IDF Vector for D1:**
D1's terms: `information (1), retrieval (1), search (1)`.
Using `idf(information) = log(4/3) ‚Čą 0.1249`, `idf(retrieval) ‚Čą 0.1249`, `idf(search) ‚Čą 0.1249`.

$$\vec{d_1} = (0.1249,\; 0.1249,\; 0.1249) \quad \text{(in the [information, retrieval, search] basis)}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ TF-IDF Vector for Query Q = "search information":**

$$\vec{q} = (0.1249,\; 0,\; 0.1249)$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Cosine Similarity:**

$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_1} = (0.1249)(0.1249) + (0)(0.1249) + (0.1249)(0.1249) = 0.0156 + 0 + 0.0156 = 0.0312$$

$$\|\vec{q}\| = \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0^2 + 0.1249^2} = \sqrt{0.0156 + 0.0156} = \sqrt{0.0312} \approx 0.1767$$

$$\|\vec{d_1}\| = \sqrt{0.1249^2 + 0.1249^2 + 0.1249^2} = \sqrt{0.0469} \approx 0.2166$$

$$\text{sim}(Q, D_1) = \frac{0.0312}{0.1767 \times 0.2166} = \frac{0.0312}{0.03827} \approx 0.8152$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Compare with Other Documents:**
- D2: contains both `search` and `information` and `retrieval` ‚Üí `sim(Q, D2) ‚Čą 0.8152` (identical TF-IDF structure to D1 in this 3-D subspace)
- D3: contains only `retrieval` from Q's terms ‚Üí low similarity
- D4: contains `search` and `information` ‚Üí `sim(Q, D4) ‚Čą 0.8152`

**Most similar documents:** D1, D2, and D4 (tied), all with cosine similarity ‚Čą **0.8152**.

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

## 5.4 Complete Model Solutions for Question B

### Solution to Question B (a)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(c \mid d) = \frac{P(c) \cdot P(d \mid c)}{P(d)}$$

For classification, we pick the class `c` that maximizes `P(c | d)`, which is equivalent to maximizing `P(c) ¬∑ P(d | c)` since `P(d)` is constant.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Na√Įve Assumption:**
Assume all words in the document are conditionally independent given the class:

$$P(d \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

Hence:

$$c^* = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid c)$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute Class Priors:**
- `P(SPAM) = 2/4 = 0.5`
- `P(HAM) = 2/4 = 0.5`

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Word Counts per Class:**

SPAM tokens (D1, D2): `cheap(1), free(2), offer(2), deal(1)` ‚Üí total = 6
HAM tokens (D3, D4): `project(2), meeting(2)` ‚Üí total = 4

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Laplace-Smoothed Conditional Probabilities:**

For SPAM (denominator = 6 + 6 = 12):

$$P(\text{cheap} \mid \text{SPAM}) = (1+1)/12 = 2/12 \approx 0.1667$$

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{SPAM}) = (2+1)/12 = 3/12 = 0.25$$

$$P(\text{offer} \mid \text{SPAM}) = (2+1)/12 = 3/12 = 0.25$$

For HAM (denominator = 4 + 6 = 10):

$$P(\text{cheap} \mid \text{HAM}) = (0+1)/10 = 1/10 = 0.1$$

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{HAM}) = (0+1)/10 = 0.1$$

$$P(\text{offer} \mid \text{HAM}) = (0+1)/10 = 0.1$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Compute Scores for Test Document d = "cheap free offer":**

Score for SPAM (using log-likelihoods):

$$\log P(\text{SPAM} \mid d) = \log(0.5) + \log(0.1667) + \log(0.25) + \log(0.25)$$

$$= -0.6931 + (-1.7918) + (-1.3863) + (-1.3863) = -5.2575$$

Score for HAM:

$$\log P(\text{HAM} \mid d) = \log(0.5) + \log(0.1) + \log(0.1) + \log(0.1)$$

$$= -0.6931 + 3 \times (-2.3026) = -0.6931 - 6.9078 = -7.6009$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Decision:**
`-5.2575 > -7.6009`, so the document is classified as **SPAM**.

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

### Solution to Question B (b)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Query Likelihood Model Definition:**

The Query Likelihood Model is a probabilistic retrieval model. It ranks documents by `P(D | Q)`, the probability that the user's query was generated from the document's language model. By Bayes' rule and assuming a uniform prior on documents, this is equivalent to ranking by `P(Q | D)`.

Under the unigram assumption:

$$P(Q \mid D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid D)$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Why Smoothing is Essential:**

Using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), `P(w | D) = 0` for any word not in `D`. This means if the query contains even a single term not in the document, the entire score becomes 0 ‚ÄĒ even if the document is otherwise very relevant. This is the **zero-probability problem**, and it makes the un-smoothed model impractical.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Example Illustrating Jelinek-Mercer Smoothing:**

Let:
- Document `D = "the analytics machine"`, so `|D| = 3`.
- Query `Q = "machine learning"`.
- `tf("machine", D) = 1`, `tf("learning", D) = 0`.
- Collection probabilities: `P("machine" | C) = 0.0001`, `P("learning" | C) = 0.00005`.
- Smoothing parameter `őĽ = 0.7`.

**Without smoothing:**

$$P(\text{learning} \mid D) = \frac{\text{tf}(\text{learning}, D)}{|D|} = \frac{0}{3} = 0$$

$$P(Q \mid D) = P(\text{machine} \mid D) \times P(\text{learning} \mid D) = \frac{1}{3} \times 0 = 0 \quad \text{‚ĚĆ False Negative}$$

**With Jelinek-Mercer smoothing:**

$$P(\text{machine} \mid D) = 0.7 \times \frac{1}{3} + 0.3 \times 0.0001 = 0.2333 + 0.00003 = 0.23333$$

$$P(\text{learning} \mid D) = 0.7 \times 0 + 0.3 \times 0.00005 = 0.000015$$

$$P(Q \mid D) = 0.23333 \times 0.000015 \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{‚úÖ Rescued!}$$

The document is no longer assigned a zero score. The term `learning`, though absent from the document, still receives a small probability from the collection model, allowing the document to be considered for ranking.

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing TF and DF:** `tf(t, d)` is the count of `t` in document `d`. `df(t)` is the number of documents in the collection containing `t`. Students frequently interchange them, leading to wrong IDF values. (Lose up to 2 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting the smoothing in Query Likelihood:** MLE alone gives zero probability for missing terms. Examiners deduct heavily if smoothing is omitted in any numerical computation.
> 3. **Not using log-likelihoods in Na√Įve Bayes:** Multiplying 10 or 20 small probabilities causes underflow. Always state the use of `log` for numerical stability.
> 4. **Confusing Stemming with Lemmatization:** Stemming is *crude* rule-based chopping. Lemmatization is *linguistic* and uses vocabulary/grammar. Examiners want this distinction.
> 5. **Inverted Index format:** A common error is writing posting lists *unsorted* by `docID`. Unsorted lists break the `O(|L1| + |L2|)` merge-intersection guarantee. Always show sorted lists.
> 6. **Na√Įve Bayes assumption not stated explicitly:** You must *explicitly state* the conditional independence assumption for full marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Print This Before the Exam:**

- ‚úÖ **Boolean Retrieval** is set-based, fast, but *unranked*. Use the **merge-intersection** algorithm on sorted posting lists for `AND`; analogous merge for `OR` and `AND NOT`.
- ‚úÖ **Inverted Index** is `term ‚Üí posting list`. Always keep posting lists **sorted by docID** for efficient set operations. Storage cost is **~10‚Äď15% of corpus size**.
- ‚úÖ **Tokenization** is NOT just "split on spaces." Handle hyphens, apostrophes, punctuation, Unicode, emails, URLs, and numbers.
- ‚úÖ **Stemming (Porter):** 5 phases of rule-based suffix removal. `sses‚Üíss`, `ies‚Üíi`, `ing‚Üí‚ąÖ`, `ed‚Üí‚ąÖ`, `ational‚Üíate`, `e‚Üí‚ąÖ` (if measure > 1).
- ‚úÖ **Lemmatization vs Stemming:** Lemmatization uses vocabulary; stemming does not. Lemmatization is slower but more accurate.
- ‚úÖ **Phrase Queries:** Use **positional index** (industry standard) or **biword index** (limited). Positional index costs ~35% extra storage.
- ‚úÖ **Vector Space Model:**
  - Weight = `tf √ó idf` where `idf = log(N / df)`.
  - Cosine similarity `= (q¬∑d) / (||q|| √ó ||d||)` ranges `[0, 1]`.
  - Documents with more query terms and rarer terms score higher.
- ‚úÖ **Finite Automaton:** `M = (Q, ő£, őī, q‚āÄ, F)`. Used in regex, tokenization rules, morphological analysis.
- ‚úÖ **Query Likelihood Model:** Rank by `P(Q | D)`. Use **smoothing** to avoid zero probabilities. Two main methods: **Jelinek-Mercer** (linear interpolation) and **Dirichlet** (Bayesian prior).
- ‚úÖ **Na√Įve Bayes Text Classification:**
  - `c* = argmax P(c) ¬∑ ‚ąŹ P(wŠĶĘ | c)`.
  - Use **Laplace (add-1) smoothing**: `P(w|c) = (count(w,c) + 1) / (ő£ count(w',c) + |V|)`.
  - Use **log-likelihoods** for numerical stability.
  - Despite the "na√Įve" independence assumption, it works remarkably well in practice.
- ‚úÖ **Key Formulas to Memorize:** TF, IDF, TF-IDF, Cosine similarity, Bayes' theorem, Na√Įve Bayes decision rule, Jelinek-Mercer, Dirichlet, Porter Stemmer rules.
- ‚úÖ **Engineering Insight:** Modern search engines (Google, Bing) combine **inverted index** (for fast candidate retrieval) with **learned ranking models** (like BERT-based neural rankers) and **language models** (for query understanding). The classical models in this module are the *building blocks*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 4 ‚Äď Sample Data Analytics Project Topics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Project 1 ‚ÄĒ Social Media Sentiment & Trend Detection System

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Sentiment Analysis & Trend Detection System** is an end-to-end data analytics pipeline that ingests unstructured textual data from social media platforms (Twitter/X, Reddit, Instagram, Facebook), applies **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **lexicon/machine-learning classifiers** to assign polarity labels (positive, negative, neutral), and applies **time-series and frequency analysis** to surface emerging keywords, hashtags, and conversation themes over a temporal window.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you own a small coffee shop and want to know what customers really think about your new cold brew. Instead of reading thousands of tweets one by one, you hire a smart assistant that:
1. **Collects** every tweet mentioning your shop (Data Collection),
2. **Sorts** them into smiley-face, frowny-face, or neutral piles (Sentiment Classification),
3. **Counts** how many of each pile appear every day (Trend Analysis),
4. **Highlights** sudden spikes ‚ÄĒ e.g., "Wow, the new oat-milk option is amazing!" ‚ÄĒ so you can act fast.

That assistant is exactly what this project builds ‚ÄĒ but at **internet scale** and in **real time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Sub-Tasks Recap (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Data Gathering** ‚Üí REST/Streaming APIs (e.g., Twitter API v2, Reddit PRAW, YouTube Data API)
> - **Preprocessing** ‚Üí Tokenisation, stop-word removal, stemming/lemmatisation, emoji/URL handling
> - **Sentiment Scoring** ‚Üí Lexicon-based (VADER, TextBlob, AFINN) **or** ML-based (Naive Bayes, SVM, LSTM, BERT)
> - **Trend Detection** ‚Üí Burst detection, sliding-window frequency, time-series decomposition
> - **Visualisation** ‚Üí Word clouds, polarity timelines, geo-maps, dashboards (Tableau / Power BI / Plotly Dash)

### 1.2 Project 2 ‚ÄĒ Topic Modelling from Social Media

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Topic Modelling** is an unsupervised **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** technique that discovers abstract "topics" ‚ÄĒ clusters of co-occurring words ‚ÄĒ that occur in a collection of documents. The system collects posts by keyword/hashtag, applies probabilistic models such as **Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)**, **Non-Negative Matrix Factorisation (NMF)**, or **BERTopic**, and visualises how each topic's prevalence evolves over time.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture a huge library with millions of unsorted letters. You don't know what each letter is about, but you notice that letters mentioning *election*, *vote*, *campaign* often appear together, and another group mentions *recipe*, *cooking*, *ingredients*. By observing which words sit near each other, the system **automatically groups** letters into themes (topics) ‚ÄĒ without ever being told the topic names. That is exactly what LDA does mathematically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Algorithms (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **LDA** (Latent Dirichlet Allocation) ‚ÄĒ generative probabilistic model
> - **NMF** (Non-negative Matrix Factorisation) ‚ÄĒ linear-algebra based
> - **BERTopic** ‚ÄĒ transformer embeddings + HDBSCAN + c-TF-IDF
> - **Top2Vec** ‚ÄĒ semantic embeddings + clustering

### 1.3 Project 3 ‚ÄĒ Student Marks Data Mining & Predictive Analytics

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Educational Data Mining (EDM)** is the application of statistical, machine-learning, and pattern-mining techniques to data originating from educational contexts. The system ingests **historical student performance records** (marks, attendance, study hours, assignments, demographics), applies **descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics** (the **DDPP** model) to uncover factors influencing academic outcomes, and provides **grade-prediction** and **at-risk-student early-warning** capabilities.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a school counsellor with 500 students but only 30 minutes per day. They cannot personally track every child's progress. Our system acts like a **super-powered assistant** that:
1. Reads every student's past report cards,
2. Spots patterns (e.g., "students who miss > 20% of classes AND submit < 50% of assignments typically score 15 marks lower"),
3. Predicts who is likely to fail the next exam,
4. Suggests targeted interventions (extra tutoring, mentoring).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The DDPP Analytics Hierarchy (Mandatory KTU Term):**
> - **Descriptive** ‚Üí "What happened?" (averages, histograms, pass-rate trends)
> - **Diagnostic** ‚Üí "Why did it happen?" (correlation, chi-square, ANOVA)
> - **Predictive** ‚Üí "What will happen?" (regression, classification, time-series)
> - **Prescriptive** ‚Üí "What should we do?" (recommendation engines, optimisation)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of Project 1 ‚ÄĒ Sentiment & Trend

### A. Lexicon-Based Sentiment Scoring
Each word $w_i$ in a text is mapped to a pre-assigned polarity score $s_i \in [-1, +1]$ from a lexicon (VADER, AFINN-165). The document-level sentiment is a weighted aggregation:

$$S_{doc} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$

where $w_i$ is the term weight (often just $1$, but TF-IDF weights improve accuracy).

### B. VADER's Compound Score
VADER outputs a **normalised compound score** $\in [-1, +1]$ using the formula:

$$\text{compound} = \frac{\sum_{i} s_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i} s_i^2 + \alpha}}$$

with $\alpha = 15$ (smoothing constant to keep denominator positive). Threshold rule: $\ge 0.05$ ‚Üí Positive; $\le -0.05$ ‚Üí Negative; otherwise Neutral.

### C. Time-Series Trend Detection
After aggregating daily sentiment counts, we apply **Linear Trend Slope** to detect drift:

$$\text{slope} = \frac{n \sum (t_i \cdot y_i) - \sum t_i \sum y_i}{n \sum t_i^2 - (\sum t_i)^2}$$

where $t_i$ is the time index and $y_i$ is the daily count or mean sentiment.

### D. Burst Detection (Kleinberg's Algorithm)
A "burst" is a sudden rise in keyword frequency modelled as a two-state Markov chain with rate ratio $r = \lambda_{\text{burst}} / \lambda_{\text{base}}$. The cost function is:

$$c(i, j) = (j-i) \cdot k \cdot \log r - \log \left[ \frac{r^{\hat{n}}}{(1+r^{\hat{n}})} \right]$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the observed count in the interval $[i, j]$.

## 2.2 Theoretical Pillars of Project 2 ‚ÄĒ Topic Modelling

### A. TF-IDF (Foundation of all topic models)
**Term Frequency:**
$$TF(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$$

**Inverse Document Frequency:**
$$IDF(t, D) = \log \frac{N}{1 + \mid\{d \in D : t \in d\}\mid}$$

**TF-IDF Score:**
$$TF\text{-}IDF(t, d, D) = TF(t, d) \times IDF(t, D)$$

### B. Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) ‚ÄĒ Generative Story
LDA assumes each document $d$ is a mixture of $K$ topics, and each topic $k$ is a distribution over the vocabulary $V$. The joint probability of a corpus is:

$$P(\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{Z}, \boldsymbol{\theta}, \boldsymbol{\phi} \mid \alpha, \beta) = \prod_{d=1}^{D} P(\theta_d \mid \alpha) \prod_{k=1}^{K} P(\phi_k \mid \beta) \prod_{n=1}^{N_d} P(z_{d,n} \mid \theta_d) \, P(w_{d,n} \mid \phi_{z_{d,n}})$$

where $\alpha$ (Dirichlet prior over topic mixtures) and $\beta$ (Dirichlet prior over word distributions) are hyperparameters.

### C. LDA Inference (Collapsed Gibbs Sampling)
The posterior probability of assigning word $w_i$ to topic $k$ is:

$$P(z_i = k \mid \mathbf{z}_{-i}, \mathbf{w}) \propto \frac{n_{d,k}^{-i} + \alpha_k}{\sum_{k'} n_{d,k'}^{-i} + \alpha_{k'}} \cdot \frac{n_{k, w_i}^{-i} + \beta_{w_i}}{\sum_{v} n_{k,v}^{-i} + \beta_{v}}$$

The first factor is the **document-topic density**, the second is the **topic-word density**.

### D. Topic Coherence Score (C_v)
The standard quality metric for a topic is:

$$C_v = \frac{2}{N \cdot (N-1)} \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} \cos(\vec{v}_{w_i}, \vec{v}_{w_j})$$

where $\vec{v}_{w}$ is the sliding-window context vector of word $w$ (typically $N = 10$ top words per topic).

## 2.3 Theoretical Pillars of Project 3 ‚ÄĒ Educational Data Mining

### A. Multiple Linear Regression for Grade Prediction

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \beta_p x_p + \varepsilon$$

The Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) solution:

$$\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}$$

### B. Logistic Regression for Pass/Fail Classification
Probability of passing:

$$P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)}}$$

Log-loss (cross-entropy) cost function:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]$$

### C. Pearson Correlation (for feature importance)
$$r_{xy} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

### D. Cosine Similarity (for student clustering)
$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}}{\vert\vert\mathbf{A}\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\mathbf{B}\vert\vert}$$

## 2.4 Consolidated KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for ESE questions on Module 4. Memorise every row.**

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Symbols** | **Use Case** |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|--------------|
| Document Polarity | $S_{doc} = \frac{\sum w_i s_i}{\sum w_i}$ | $w_i$=weight, $s_i$=lexicon score | Lexicon sentiment |
| VADER Compound | $c = \frac{\sum s_i}{\sqrt{\sum s_i^2 + 15}}$ | $\alpha = 15$ smoothing | Single-score sentiment |
| Trend Slope | $m = \frac{n \sum t y - \sum t \sum y}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$ | $t$=time, $y$=count | Linear drift detection |
| Term Frequency | $TF(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum f_{t',d}}$ | $f$=raw count | Word importance |
| Inverse Doc Freq | $IDF(t,D) = \log \frac{N}{1 + \vert\{d:t \in d\}\vert}$ | $N$=corpus size | Rare-word weighting |
| LDA Gibbs | $P(z_i=k) \propto (n_{d,k}+\alpha)(n_{k,w}+\beta)$ | $\alpha,\beta$ priors | Topic assignment |
| Topic Coherence | $C_v = \frac{2}{N(N-1)} \sum \cos(\vec{v}_{w_i},\vec{v}_{w_j})$ | $N$=top-10 words | Topic quality |
| OLS Regression | $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | $X$=features, $y$=target | Grade prediction |
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $z$=linear input | Pass/fail prob. |
| Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{A \cdot B}{\vert\vert A \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert B \vert\vert}$ | $A,B$=vectors | Student clustering |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Confusion matrix terms | Classifier eval |
| F1 Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | $P$=precision, $R$=recall | Imbalanced data |
| RMSE | $RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Prediction error | Regression eval |
| Time Series Decomp. | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ | Trend+Seasonal+Cyc+Irreg | Score trend analysis |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation ‚ÄĒ TF-IDF (Foundation of Topic Modelling)

We derive the rationale behind TF-IDF from first principles.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the goal.**
We need a numerical weight for every word $w$ in every document $d$ such that words highly specific to $d$ receive high weight, while common words ("the", "is", "a") receive low weight.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Start with raw Term Frequency.**
A word that appears 10 times is more important than one appearing once:
$$TF(t, d) = f_{t,d}$$

But raw counts are unfair to long documents. **Normalise** by total words in $d$:
$$TF(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Penalise common words using IDF.**
If a word appears in *every* document, it carries no discriminative power. Count documents containing the term: $\vert\{d \in D : t \in d\}\vert$. Divide total documents $N$ by this count:

$$\text{ratio} = \frac{N}{\vert\{d \in D : t \in d\}\vert}$$

Take the logarithm (compresses the wide range; classic IDF form is $\log_{10}$, but $\log_e$ is equivalent up to a constant):

$$IDF(t, D) = \log \frac{N}{1 + \vert\{d \in D : t \in d\}\vert}$$

The `+1` inside the denominator is **Laplace smoothing** to prevent division by zero when a term is absent from the corpus.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Combine multiplicatively.**
A word must be both **frequent in the document** AND **rare in the corpus** to score high:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TF\text{-}IDF(t, d, D) &= TF(t, d) \times IDF(t, D) \\
&= \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} \times \log \frac{N}{1 + \vert\{d \in D : t \in d\}\vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Multiplication (not addition) ensures that a term scoring zero in either factor (e.g., a common word with $IDF \approx 0$, or a missing word with $TF = 0$) is correctly assigned a final weight of zero.

## 3.2 Derivation ‚ÄĒ OLS Solution for Grade Prediction (Project 3)

We want the line $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ that minimises the sum of squared residuals.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Define the residual sum of squares (RSS).**
$$RSS(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\right)^2$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate with respect to $\beta_0$.**
$$\frac{\partial RSS}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0$$

This gives the constraint: $\sum y_i = n \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum x_i$, so the line passes through the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate with respect to $\beta_1$.**
$$\frac{\partial RSS}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum x_i (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0$$

This gives: $\sum x_i y_i = \beta_0 \sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^2$.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve the 2-equation system.**
Substituting $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i y_i &= (\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}) \sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^2 \\
\sum x_i y_i - n \bar{x} \bar{y} &= \beta_1 \left( \sum x_i^2 - n \bar{x}^2 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Final closed-form slope.**
$$\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Matrix form for $p$ features.**
Stacking $n$ equations gives $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$. Setting the gradient of $RSS = \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} ((\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})) &= 0 \\
-2 \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}) &= 0 \\
\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} &= \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y} \\
\boldsymbol{\beta} &= (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1}$ exists only when features are **linearly independent** (no perfect multicollinearity). This is a critical assumption that students often miss in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Derivation ‚ÄĒ Logistic Regression Decision Rule

Linear regression outputs unbounded values, but grade outcomes are bounded probabilities $[0, 1]$. We squash the linear combination with the sigmoid.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Linear combination.**
$$z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the sigmoid function.**
$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

Verify bounds: $z \to +\infty \Rightarrow \sigma \to 1$; $z \to -\infty \Rightarrow \sigma \to 0$; $z = 0 \Rightarrow \sigma = 0.5$.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Threshold to get a class label.**
$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE).**
Assuming independence, the likelihood of $m$ training samples is:

$$L(\mathbf{w}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \hat{y}_i^{y_i} (1 - \hat{y}_i)^{1-y_i}$$

Take the log-likelihood to convert the product into a sum (easier to optimise):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell(\mathbf{w}) &= \log L(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximise $\ell$ ‚áĒ minimise the negative, giving the **cross-entropy / log-loss**:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the function that gradient descent minimises in every logistic-regression library (scikit-learn's `LogisticRegression`, TensorFlow, etc.).

## 3.4 Derivation ‚ÄĒ VADER Compound Score

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ VADER computes valence scores** for each word: $s_i \in [-4, +4]$.
For example: "amazing" ‚Üí $+3.2$, "horrible" ‚Üí $-2.6$, "okay" ‚Üí $+0.5$.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply intensification and negation rules** (e.g., "not good" ‚Üí negate, "very good" ‚Üí boost by 25-50%).

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Sum all adjusted scores:**
$$S_{sum} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Normalise to $[-1, +1]$ using the Frobenius norm trick.**

Why $\sqrt{\sum s_i^2 + 15}$? Because dividing by the Euclidean norm of the score vector projects onto the unit ball, ensuring the result is always within $[-1, +1]$ regardless of how many words there are. The constant 15 prevents a single-word output from collapsing to 1 (which would lose the rank order).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{compound} &= \frac{S_{sum}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i^2 + \alpha}}, \quad \alpha = 15 \\
&= \frac{\sum s_i}{\sqrt{\sum s_i^2 + 15}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Apply the standard KTU threshold rule:**
$$\text{Label} = \begin{cases} \text{Positive} & \text{if } c \geq +0.05 \\ \text{Negative} & \text{if } c \leq -0.05 \\ \text{Neutral} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix ‚ÄĒ Which Algorithm to Use?

| **Project Scenario** | **Recommended Algorithm** | **Justification** | **Key Hyperparameter** |
|----------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Twitter sentiment (short, noisy) | VADER / DistilBERT | VADER handles emojis & slang; DistilBERT for accuracy | `max_length=128` |
| Product reviews (long, formal) | Fine-tuned BERT / LSTM | Longer context, domain-specific vocab | `batch_size=32` |
| Topic discovery (large corpus) | LDA / NMF | Unsupervised, interpretable | `n_topics=10`, `alpha=0.1` |
| Topic discovery (small corpus) | BERTopic | Uses embeddings, works with few docs | `min_topic_size=15` |
| Grade prediction (continuous) | Linear Regression / Random Forest | Interpretable, handles non-linearity | `n_estimators=100` |
| Pass/Fail classification | Logistic Regression / XGBoost | Probabilistic, robust to imbalance | `class_weight='balanced'` |
| Student clustering | K-Means + Cosine Similarity | Groups similar learners | `k=5` clusters |
| Trend detection (spikes) | Kleinberg burst / Prophet | Designed for time-series anomalies | `changepoint_prior_scale=0.05` |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]**
> Define **Sentiment Analysis** and list any **three lexicon-based tools** used for it. State the **VADER compound score range** and the threshold used to label a text as Positive.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining) is the computational study of opinions, sentiments, and emotions expressed in text. It classifies a document as **Positive, Negative, or Neutral** based on polarity.
- **Three lexicon tools (1.5 marks):** VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner), AFINN-165, TextBlob, Bing Liu Lexicon, SentiWordNet.
- **VADER range & threshold (0.5 marks):** Compound score $\in [-1, +1]$; threshold $\pm 0.05$.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Jun 2024]**
> What is **Topic Modelling**? State the **Dirichlet priors** used in LDA and explain their role in **one line each**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Topic Modelling is an unsupervised NLP technique that automatically discovers hidden thematic structure (topics) in a collection of documents by clustering co-occurring words.
- **Dirichlet priors (1 mark each):**
  - **$\alpha$** ‚ÄĒ Prior over the document-topic distribution $\theta_d$; smaller $\alpha$ ‚Üí each document contains fewer dominant topics.
  - **$\beta$** ‚ÄĒ Prior over the topic-word distribution $\phi_k$; smaller $\beta$ ‚Üí each topic contains fewer dominant words.

## Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 marks) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2024-style]**
> **(a)** Design a complete **architecture** for a social-media **Sentiment & Trend Detection System** targeting a brand like "Boat" (audio wearables). Explain each block of the pipeline with the **specific algorithm/tool** you would deploy at every stage. Justify your design choices. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the **VADER compound score** for the review:
> *"The headphones are not bad at all, but the battery life is truly horrible and disappointing."*
> Use the lexicon values: `bad = -1.5`, `horrible = -2.6`, `disappointing = -1.8`, `battery = 0.0`, `truly = 0.2` (intensifier, multiply next word by 1.5). Apply VADER's negation rule ("not bad" ‚Üí flip sign of the next word). Use $\alpha = 15$. Classify the final compound score using the standard threshold. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Architecture (7 marks):**
| **Block** | **Tool/Algorithm** | **Justification (1 mark each)** |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1. Data Ingestion | Twitter API v2 (Filtered Stream), Reddit PRAW, YouTube Data API | Real-time streaming for brand mentions; multi-platform coverage. |
| 2. Storage | MongoDB Atlas (NoSQL) or AWS S3 (raw JSON) + PostgreSQL (structured) | MongoDB handles unstructured JSON; PostgreSQL for aggregated analytics. |
| 3. Preprocessing | NLTK / spaCy pipeline: tokeniser ‚Üí stop-word removal ‚Üí lemmatiser ‚Üí emoji-to-text conversion (`emoji` library) ‚Üí URL/mention stripping | Standard NLP cleaning; emoji handling crucial for social media. |
| 4. Sentiment Engine | **Hybrid:** VADER for short tweets + fine-tuned **DistilBERT** (Hugging Face) for long reviews | VADER is fast and handles slang/emojis; DistilBERT gives state-of-the-art accuracy. |
| 5. Aggregation | Daily/weekly rollup with Pandas groupby | Time-series analysis needs aligned timestamps. |
| 6. Trend Detection | **Kleinberg burst algorithm** + **Facebook Prophet** for seasonality | Captures both sudden spikes and periodic patterns (weekend vs weekday). |
| 7. Visualisation | **Plotly Dash** interactive dashboard with word cloud, polarity timeline, geo-map | Stakeholders need self-service exploration. |
| 8. Alerting | Slack/Email webhook when negative sentiment spike > 2ŌÉ | Real-time crisis detection. |

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ VADER Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify all scored tokens and apply rules left-to-right.
- "not" ‚Üí flag negation on next word.
- "bad" ‚Üí $-1.5$, but negation flips it ‚Üí $+1.5$.
- "truly" ‚Üí intensifier (1.5√ó multiplier on next scored word).
- "horrible" ‚Üí $-2.6 \times 1.5 = -3.9$.
- "disappointing" ‚Üí $-1.8$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Sum of adjusted scores:
$$S_{sum} = (+1.5) + 0 + (-3.9) + (-1.8) = -4.2$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Sum of squares (for denominator):
$$\sum s_i^2 = (1.5)^2 + (-3.9)^2 + (-1.8)^2 = 2.25 + 15.21 + 3.24 = 20.7$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply VADER formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{compound} &= \frac{-4.2}{\sqrt{20.7 + 15}} \\
&= \frac{-4.2}{\sqrt{35.7}} \\
&= \frac{-4.2}{5.975} \approx -0.703
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply threshold $-0.05$. Since $-0.703 \leq -0.05$, the review is classified as **NEGATIVE** ‚úď.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Interpretation ‚ÄĒ Despite the negation saving "bad", the strong intensifier-amplified "horrible" and "disappointing" dominate, leading to a strongly negative verdict. The model correctly captures linguistic nuance.

---

> **Question B (14 marks) ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Jun 2024-style, Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** Design a complete **Topic Modelling pipeline** for analysing 50,000 tweets about "#AIinEducation". List the **preprocessing steps**, choose an **algorithm (LDA, NMF, or BERTopic)** with justification, state the **mathematical formulation** of your chosen model, and explain how you would **evaluate topic quality**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A student's performance dataset yields the regression equation:
> $$\hat{y} = 12.5 + 0.45 x_1 + 0.60 x_2 - 0.30 x_3$$
> where $x_1$ = study hours/week, $x_2$ = attendance %, $x_3$ = no. of backlogs. Predict the marks for a student with $(x_1, x_2, x_3) = (20, 85, 1)$. Comment on the **practical feasibility** of using this single equation for a 500-student batch, and state **two limitations** of multiple linear regression in this context. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Topic Modelling Pipeline (7 marks):**

| **Step** | **Detail (marks distribution)** |
|----------|---------------------------------|
| 1. Data Collection | Twitter API v2 with `#AIinEducation` filter, 50k tweets, save as JSONL (0.5 mark) |
| 2. Preprocessing | Lowercasing, URL/@mention removal, hashtag splitting, emoji conversion, stop-word removal, lemmatisation, **bigram/trigram detection** using Gensim Phrases (1 mark) |
| 3. Tokenisation | Gensim `simple_preprocess`, filter extremes (`no_below=20`, `no_above=0.5`) (0.5 mark) |
| 4. Algorithm Choice | **BERTopic** (modern, embedding-based) or **LDA** (classical, interpretable). **Recommendation: LDA** for academic context & interpretability (1 mark) |
| 5. Mathematical Formulation | LDA joint probability with priors $\alpha$ (doc-topic) and $\beta$ (topic-word). Collapsed Gibbs Sampling for inference (2 marks) |
| 6. Hyperparameter Tuning | Grid search over $K \in \{5, 10, 15, 20\}$, $\alpha \in \{0.01, 0.1, 1.0\}$; choose by max $C_v$ coherence (1 mark) |
| 7. Evaluation | **Topic Coherence $C_v$**, **Topic Diversity**, **PyLDAvis inter-topic distance map**, human expert review of top-10 words per topic (1 mark) |

**LDA Joint Distribution (2 marks):**
$$P(\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{Z}, \boldsymbol{\theta}, \boldsymbol{\phi} \mid \alpha, \beta) = \prod_{d=1}^{D} P(\theta_d \mid \alpha) \prod_{k=1}^{K} P(\phi_k \mid \beta) \prod_{n=1}^{N_d} P(z_{d,n} \mid \theta_d) P(w_{d,n} \mid \phi_{z_{d,n}})$$

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Regression Prediction & Critique (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Plug in values:
$$\hat{y} = 12.5 + 0.45(20) + 0.60(85) - 0.30(1)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute each term:
- $0.45 \times 20 = 9.0$
- $0.60 \times 85 = 51.0$
- $0.30 \times 1 = 0.30$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Sum:
$$\hat{y} = 12.5 + 9.0 + 51.0 - 0.30 = 72.2 \text{ marks}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Feasibility comment ‚ÄĒ The single equation **assumes the same relationship holds for all 500 students**, ignoring subject heterogeneity (CS vs Civil), branch difficulty, and individual learning speeds. It is a **first-cut approximation** but not a personalised predictor.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Two limitations of OLS Multiple Linear Regression:
1. **Linearity assumption** ‚ÄĒ Grade vs study hours is often non-linear (diminishing returns after 40 hrs).
2. **Multicollinearity** ‚ÄĒ Attendance and study hours are correlated; inflated variance of $\beta$ estimates.
3. **Homoscedasticity** ‚ÄĒ Residual variance should be constant; in real data, high performers cluster tightly while low performers are noisy.
4. **Outlier sensitivity** ‚ÄĒ A few exceptional students can drag the line and bias predictions for the majority.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Laplace smoothing** (`+1`) in the IDF denominator ‚Üí loses 1 mark in any derivation question.
> 2. **Confusing $C_v$ and UMass coherence** ‚ÄĒ they measure the same thing (topic quality) but with **opposite signs**; UMass is *higher-is-worse*, $C_v$ is *higher-is-better*.
> 3. **Not stating the threshold $\pm 0.05$** for VADER ‚Üí 0.5 mark deduction even if compound score is correct.
> 4. **Inverting the OLS formula** ‚ÄĒ writing $\beta = (X^T y)^{-1} X^T X$ instead of the correct $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$.
> 5. **Ignoring the `no_above` and `no_below` filters** in Gensim tokenisation ‚Üí LDA becomes dominated by either ultra-rare or super-common words.
> 6. **Saying "LDA is supervised"** ‚ÄĒ instant 0 in any topic-modelling question. It is **unsupervised**.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 Project Topics ‚ÄĒ Last-Minute Revision Bullet Bank:**

### Project 1 ‚ÄĒ Sentiment & Trend Detection
- ‚úÖ Sentiment = Positive / Negative / Neutral (sometimes Intensity, Emotion).
- ‚úÖ VADER compound $\in [-1, +1]$; threshold $\pm 0.05$; $\alpha = 15$ in formula.
- ‚úÖ Lexicon tools: VADER, AFINN, TextBlob, SentiWordNet.
- ‚úÖ ML tools: Naive Bayes, SVM, LSTM, BERT family.
- ‚úÖ Trend detection algorithms: Linear slope, Kleinberg burst, Prophet, ARIMA.
- ‚úÖ Visualisation: Word cloud, polarity timeline, geo-map, dashboard.

### Project 2 ‚ÄĒ Topic Modelling
- ‚úÖ Topic modelling is **unsupervised** NLP.
- ‚úÖ **TF-IDF** = Term Frequency √ó Inverse Document Frequency; uses Laplace smoothing.
- ‚úÖ **LDA** uses Dirichlet priors $\alpha$ (doc-topic) and $\beta$ (topic-word).
- ‚úÖ **NMF** is a linear-algebra alternative; faster but less probabilistic.
- ‚úÖ **BERTopic** uses transformer embeddings + HDBSCAN clustering.
- ‚úÖ Evaluation: **Topic Coherence $C_v$** (higher is better), Topic Diversity, human review.
- ‚úÖ Time-trend visualisation: stacked area chart, line chart per topic.

### Project 3 ‚ÄĒ Student Marks Mining
- ‚úÖ Analytics hierarchy: **Descriptive ‚Üí Diagnostic ‚Üí Predictive ‚Üí Prescriptive** (DDPP).
- ‚úÖ **Linear Regression** for continuous grade prediction; OLS solution $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$.
- ‚úÖ **Logistic Regression** for pass/fail; sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$.
- ‚úÖ **Pearson correlation** for feature-target relationship; **cosine similarity** for clustering.
- ‚úÖ Evaluation: RMSE, MAE, R¬≤ for regression; Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 for classification.
- ‚úÖ Limitations of MLR: linearity, multicollinearity, homoscedasticity, outliers.

### Cross-Cutting (All 3 Projects)
- ‚úÖ All projects share a **CRISP-DM** style pipeline: Business Understanding ‚Üí Data ‚Üí Preprocessing ‚Üí Modelling ‚Üí Evaluation ‚Üí Deployment.
- ‚úÖ All projects need **data ethics**: GDPR, IRB approval, anonymisation.
- ‚úÖ All projects require **scalable storage**: pandas ‚Üí Dask ‚Üí Spark.
- ‚úÖ All projects end with **stakeholder-facing dashboards**, not raw models.
- ‚úÖ KTU frequently asks: *"Compare two algorithms"* and *"Justify your choice with the maths"* ‚ÄĒ always quote a formula in such answers.
